```mermaid
    D --> E[Redrawn Part with Modifications]
    E --> F[Reapply Tolerance and Cost Analysis]
    F --> G{Cost Reduced or Quality Improved?}
    G -- No --> H[Iterate Further]
    H --> D
    G -- Yes --> I[Document Redesign with Justification]
    I --> J[Submit to Faculty or Release to Production]
```

**Typical violations a KTU student will encounter in a 14-mark redesign question:**

1. Sharp internal corners (radius 0 mm) — must be replaced with $r \ge 1.6$ mm.
2. Deep narrow pockets — must be redesigned as stepped or open slots.
3. Non-standard hole sizes — must be rounded to nearest standard drill.
4. Multiple setups implied by part orientation — must be re-oriented to single-setup.
5. Excessively tight tolerances on non-functional features — must be relaxed per 10× rule.
6. Material with poor machinability — must be substituted or pre-heat-treated.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]

**"List any six design considerations that a designer must follow to make a component suitable for machining. Briefly justify each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — ~80 words):**

1. **Avoid sharp internal corners** — Standard end mills cannot produce 0 mm internal radius; always specify a fillet $\ge$ tool nose radius (e.g., $r = 0.8, 1.6, 3.2$ mm).
2. **Standardise hole sizes** — Use ISO drill series (e.g., Ø6, Ø8, Ø10 mm) to avoid custom tools.
3. **Minimise number of setups** — Design features accessible from one orientation to cut locating errors and re-fixturing time.
4. **Specify machining allowances** — Provide 2–5 mm on castings/forgings for finish cut.
5. **Apply the 10× tolerance rule** — Tighter on functional, looser on non-functional features.
6. **Choose free-machining materials** — AISI 1215 (machinability 100%) over AISI 304 SS (21%) when possible.

*(1 mark for naming 6 considerations, 1 mark for justification, 1 mark for example values/diagrams.)*

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**"What is the 10× rule in tolerance specification, and why is it applied? Illustrate with a numerical example."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 10× rule states that **tolerances on non-critical features should be 10 times looser** than the critical functional feature. It is applied to **control manufacturing cost**, because tighter tolerances exponentially increase machining time, tool wear, and rejection rates.

**Numerical example:** A bearing journal requires $\pm 0.05$ mm. The adjacent shaft shoulder (non-functional) should be $\pm 0.5$ mm. A general body diameter (non-functional) can be $\pm 1$ mm. The cost of holding $\pm 0.05$ mm is approximately 100× the cost of holding $\pm 0.5$ mm.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for rationale, 1 mark for example with cost factor.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions are typically structured as **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**, often combining (a) a design/redesign exercise with (b) a numerical cost or tolerance problem.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [Choice 1]**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 1]**

A steel bracket shown in the conceptual description below is to be redesigned for **single-setup CNC milling on a 3-axis VMC**.

**Original Part Description:**
- Base plate: 120 mm × 80 mm × 12 mm, with four mounting holes at the corners (Ø8 mm, ±0.1 mm, $R_a$ = 1.6 µm).
- Central pocket: 60 mm × 40 mm × 8 mm deep, **with sharp internal corners (0 mm radius)**.
- Two side slots: 20 mm wide × 30 mm long × 10 mm deep, on the top face.
- One central hole: Ø20 mm, ±0.02 mm, $R_a$ = 0.8 µm.
- Material: AISI 304 Stainless Steel.

**Sub-parts:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Identify **at least five DFMch violations** in the original design and **redesign** the part, with justifications. Draw a neat sketch of the redesigned bracket.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the redesigned part, calculate the **machining time** for the central pocket operation using a Ø10 mm 4-flute HSS end mill, given: $v = 25$ m/min, $f_z = 0.04$ mm/tooth, $a_e = 35$ mm, $a_p = 8$ mm. Also estimate the **cost per part** assuming machine rate $C_t =$ ₹4/min, tool cost $C_{tool} =$ ₹80, tool change time $T_h = 1.5$ min, and tool life at the given speed is 45 min.

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a) [7 marks]

**Violations identified and redesigns:**

| # | Original (Violation) | Redesign | Justification (DFMch Rule) |
|---|----------------------|----------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Sharp 0 mm internal corners in pocket | Specify $r = 5$ mm corner radius (≥ half of Ø10 tool) | Rule 1: Avoid sharp internal corners |
| 2 | AISI 304 SS — poor machinability (21%) | Change to AISI 1020 steel **or** AISI 1215 free-cutting steel | Rule 10: Choose free-machining materials |
| 3 | Ø20 mm hole tolerance ±0.02 mm — possibly over-specified for a generic central feature | Apply 10× rule → ±0.05 mm if not a critical bearing fit | Rule 7: Tolerance stacking & datum hierarchy |
| 4 | Base thickness 12 mm — thin for a 60 × 40 pocket; risk of deflection | Increase base thickness to 16 mm **or** add a central rib for rigidity | Rule 4: Tool length & rigidity |
| 5 | Four mounting holes + side slots + central hole + pocket → 4+ setups | Re-orient design so all features accessible from top face → **single setup** | Rule 3: Minimise number of setups |

**Neat sketch** (described for KTU board): Top view showing:
- 120 × 80 × 16 mm base (thicker).
- Central pocket 60 × 40 × 8 with $r = 5$ mm fillets at four corners.
- Two slots 20 × 30 × 10 on top face, oriented along X-axis.
- Central hole Ø20 with looser tolerance.
- Four corner holes Ø8, ±0.2 mm, $R_a$ = 3.2 µm (loosened).

*(Mark split: 2 marks for identifying violations, 3 marks for redesign with sketch, 2 marks for justification linking to DFMch rules.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $v = 25$ m/min, $f_z = 0.04$ mm/tooth, $z = 4$, $D = 10$ mm, $a_e = 35$ mm, $a_p = 8$ mm.

**Step 1 — Spindle speed:**
$$N = \dfrac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 10} = \dfrac{25000}{31.416} \approx 795.8 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Table feed:**
$$f_r = f_z \cdot z \cdot N = 0.04 \times 4 \times 795.8 = 127.3 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3 — MRR:**
$$Z_w = a_e \cdot a_p \cdot f_r = 35 \times 8 \times 127.3 = 35{,}644 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \approx 594 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Feed length for pocket** (60 mm long, but with 35 mm engagement per pass, the tool must traverse in **two passes** of 30 mm each, plus approach/overtravel):

$$L_{per\,pass} = 30 + 5 + 5 = 40 \text{ mm (approx, including 5 mm approach and 5 mm overtravel)}$$

Total feed length for two passes:
$$L_{total} = 2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Machining time:**
$$T_m = \dfrac{L_{total}}{f_r} = \dfrac{80}{127.3} \approx 0.628 \text{ min} \approx 37.7 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — Number of parts per tool:**
$$N_p = \dfrac{T}{T_m} = \dfrac{45}{0.628} \approx 71.7 \approx 71 \text{ parts}$$

**Step 7 — Cost per part:**
$$C_{part} = C_t \cdot T_m + C_t \cdot T_h \cdot \dfrac{1}{N_p} + \dfrac{C_{tool}}{N_p}$$

$$C_{part} = 4 \times 0.628 + 4 \times 1.5 \times \dfrac{1}{71} + \dfrac{80}{71}$$

$$C_{part} = 2.512 + 0.0845 + 1.1268$$

$$C_{part} \approx \text{₹}3.72 \text{ per part}$$

*(Mark split: 2 marks for spindle speed & feed, 2 marks for MRR & time, 1 mark for $N_p$, 2 marks for cost with all components.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Choice 2]**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper 2024, Module 1]**

A cast iron housing has the following machined features, in order of manufacturing:

1. **Top face** — 100 mm × 80 mm, finish-machined to $R_a$ = 1.6 µm, flatness 0.05 mm.
2. **Central bore** — Ø40 H7 (+0.025/0), $R_a$ = 0.8 µm.
3. **Four bolt holes** — Ø8 mm, ±0.2 mm, $R_a$ = 6.3 µm, on a 70 mm × 50 mm PCD.
4. **Side slot** — 15 mm wide × 20 mm deep, open on one end, with $r = 0.5$ mm at the closed end.

**Sub-parts:**

**(a) [7 marks]** Identify the **critical functional features** and apply the **10× rule** to derive appropriate tolerances for each non-critical feature. Justify the assignment of ISO surface roughness grades.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the central bore (Ø40 H7), a boring operation is selected. Compute the **optimum cutting speed** using Taylor's equation $v \cdot T^{0.30} = 350$, given $T_h = 1.2$ min, $T_m = 0.8$ min, $C_t =$ ₹6/min, $C_{tool} =$ ₹120. Comment on the choice of $v_{opt}$ for cast iron.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Identify critical features (function-driven):**
- **Top face** (sealing surface for gasket) — critical flatness.
- **Central bore** (bearing seat) — critical for H7 fit.
- **Bolt holes** (assembly fasteners) — moderately critical.
- **Side slot** (cable/keyway) — least critical.

**Step 2 — Apply 10× rule:**

| Feature | Critical? | Tolerance Assigned | Surface Roughness $R_a$ |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| Top face flatness | Yes | 0.05 mm | 1.6 µm (gasket seal) |
| Central bore Ø40 H7 | Yes | +0.025/0 mm (per ISO) | 0.8 µm (bearing fit) |
| Bolt holes Ø8 | No (10× rule) | ±0.5 mm (10 × 0.05) | 6.3 µm (clearance fit) |
| Side slot width 15 mm | No (10× rule) | ±0.5 mm (or ISO 2768-mK) | 6.3 µm (non-contact) |

**Step 3 — ISO Surface Roughness Justification:**

- **N1–N4 grade** ($R_a$ 0.025–0.2 µm): Cylinder bores of high-precision bearings.
- **N5 grade** ($R_a$ 0.4–0.8 µm): Bearing seats, hydraulic cylinder bores.
- **N6 grade** ($R_a$ 0.8–1.6 µm): General machined faces, sealing surfaces.
- **N7–N8 grade** ($R_a$ 3.2–6.3 µm): Non-contact surfaces, bolt holes, keyways.
- **N9–N11 grade** ($R_a$ 12.5–50 µm): As-forged/cast, rough surfaces.

**Conclusion:** The top face (N6) and central bore (N5) require grinding/finish turning; the bolt holes and slot (N7) need only standard drilling/milling.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for identifying critical features, 2 marks for applying 10× rule with table, 1 mark for ISO grade justification, 2 marks for assignment rationale.)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Given:** Taylor's equation: $v \cdot T^{0.30} = 350$, so $C = 350$, $n = 0.30$.

Other data: $T_h = 1.2$ min, $T_m = 0.8$ min, $C_t =$ ₹6/min, $C_{tool} =$ ₹120.

**Step 1 — Optimum cutting speed formula (minimum cost):**

$$v_{opt} = \dfrac{C}{\left[\dfrac{(1-n)}{n} \cdot \dfrac{C_t \cdot T_h + C_{tool}}{C_t \cdot T_m}\right]^n}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the term $\dfrac{(1-n)}{n}$:**

$$\dfrac{(1-n)}{n} = \dfrac{1 - 0.30}{0.30} = \dfrac{0.70}{0.30} = 2.333$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\dfrac{C_t T_h + C_{tool}}{C_t T_m}$:**

$$C_t T_h + C_{tool} = 6 \times 1.2 + 120 = 7.2 + 120 = 127.2$$

$$C_t T_m = 6 \times 0.8 = 4.8$$

$$\dfrac{127.2}{4.8} = 26.5$$

**Step 4 — Inside the bracket:**

$$2.333 \times 26.5 = 61.82$$

**Step 5 — Raise to power $n = 0.30$:**

$$61.82^{0.30} = e^{0.30 \cdot \ln(61.82)} = e^{0.30 \times 4.124} = e^{1.237} \approx 3.446$$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$v_{opt} = \dfrac{350}{3.446} \approx 101.6 \text{ m/min}$$

**Step 7 — Corresponding optimum tool life:**

$$T_{opt} = \left(\dfrac{C}{v_{opt}}\right)^{1/n} = \left(\dfrac{350}{101.6}\right)^{1/0.30} = (3.445)^{3.333}$$

$$3.445^{3.333} = e^{3.333 \times \ln(3.445)} = e^{3.333 \times 1.237} = e^{4.124} \approx 61.82 \text{ min}$$

**Step 8 — Comment on cast iron:**

Cast iron (gray or nodular) is **dry-machined** with **ceramic or carbide inserts** at speeds typically **80–200 m/min**. A calculated $v_{opt} = 101.6$ m/min lies comfortably in the recommended range, validating the selection. The relatively **high $n = 0.30$** indicates a **steep tool-life-vs-speed curve** — small speed deviations cause large tool-life changes, so the designer/operator must adhere strictly to $v_{opt}$ in production.

*(Mark split: 2 marks for formula, 2 marks for numerical substitution, 1 mark for $v_{opt}$ result, 1 mark for $T_{opt}$, 1 mark for comment on cast iron machinability.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost KTU Students 2–4 Marks Per Question:**

1. **Confusing "machining allowance" with "tolerance"** — Allowance is *extra material to be removed*; tolerance is *permissible variation*. Examiners deduct 1 full mark if these are interchanged.

2. **Writing "$R_a$ = 0.8" without units** — Surface roughness is meaningless without **µm or µm Ra**. Always write $R_a = 0.8 \, \mu m$ or $R_a = 0.8 \, \mu m \, Ra$.

3. **Using "tolerance" and "accuracy" interchangeably** — Tolerance is the *permitted deviation*; accuracy is the *closeness to true value*. They are not synonyms.

4. **Forgetting to multiply feed by number of teeth in milling** — A common error is to use $f_r = N \cdot f_z$ instead of $f_r = N \cdot z \cdot f_z$. This makes the answer 4× too low and the cost 4× too high. KTU examiners mark this **wrong, no partial credit**.

5. **Not specifying units for cutting speed** — Always **m/min** (not m/s, not mm/s). The formula $N = 1000v/(\pi D)$ is calibrated for $v$ in m/min and $D$ in mm.

6. **Redesigning without a sketch** — A 14-mark question **explicitly asks** for a neat sketch. Skipping the sketch costs a **flat 2 marks** regardless of textual quality.

7. **Ignoring datum hierarchy** — If the question mentions a bearing bore, the student **must** designate it as **Datum A** and reference all other tolerances to it. Failure to do this loses 1–2 marks.

8. **Cost calculation without including tool-change time** — The term $C_t \cdot T_h$ is often missed. Examiners check for all three terms: machining time, tool-change time (amortised), and tool cost (amortised).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Component Design for Machining**

- [ ] **DFMch Goal:** Minimise machining cost, setups, tooling, and time *while meeting functional specs*.
- [ ] **12 Golden Rules:** Avoid sharp internal corners, standardise chamfers/radii, minimise setups, use standard tool lengths, avoid narrow pockets, specify allowances, define datums, combine features, prefer flat perpendicular faces, use free-machining materials, standardise hole sizes, avoid undercuts.
- [ ] **10× Tolerance Rule:** $t_{non-critical} = 10 \times t_{critical}$ — every step away from a critical feature = 10× looser tolerance.
- [ ] **ISO Surface Grades:** N5 = bearing fit (0.4–0.8 µm), N6 = sealing (0.8–1.6 µm), N7 = general (3.2 µm), N8 = non-contact (6.3 µm).
- [ ] **Machining Allowance:** Sand casting 2–5 mm, die casting 0.5–1.5 mm, hot forging 1.5–3 mm, cold forging 0.3–1 mm.
- [ ] **MRR Formula:** $Z_w = v \cdot f \cdot d$ (turning) or $Z_w = a_e \cdot a_p \cdot f_r$ (milling).
- [ ] **Cutting Velocity:** $v = \pi D N / (60 \times 1000)$ in m/min with $D$ in mm.
- [ ] **Milling Feed:** $f_r = f_z \cdot z \cdot N$ — **never** forget the $z$ multiplier.
- [ ] **Taylor's Tool Life:** $v \cdot T^n = C$ — opt cost speed uses $n$ in the exponent.
- [ ] **Cost Equation:** $C_{part} = C_t T_m + (C_t T_h + C_{tool}) \cdot (T_m / T)$ — three terms must all appear.
- [ ] **Datum Hierarchy:** Always designate a primary datum; functional feature first.
- [ ] **Sketch Required:** Every 14-mark redesign question must include a **neat, dimensioned, labelled sketch** of the modified part.
- [ ] **Units Discipline:** $v$ in m/min, $f$ in mm/rev or mm/tooth, $D$ in mm, $Z_w$ in mm³/min or mm³/s, $R_a$ in µm, tolerances in mm (or µm for precision).
- [ ] **Material Machinability:** AISI 1215 = 100% (reference), AISI 1020 = 70%, AISI 1040 = 60%, AISI 304 SS = 21%, Inconel 718 = 12% — choose the highest that meets functional needs.
- [ ] **Redesign Mindset:** When the shop floor says "this feature is impossible/expensive to machine," **redesign the feature**, not the process.
- [ ] **Cost-of-Tolerance Rule:** $C_{tol} \propto (1/t)^{1.5}$ — halving the tolerance increases cost by ~2.8×.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Component Design for Machining: Considerations and Redesign Examples**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Form Design of Castings and Weldments: Design principles for castings and weldments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Form Design of Castings and Weldments: Design principles for castings and weldments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Form Design for Castings and Weldments?

**Form Design** refers to the engineering discipline of shaping a component's geometry right at the conceptual stage so that it can be **manufactured economically, accurately, and reliably** through a specific primary shaping process — in this module, **Casting** and **Welding**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> *"Form design is the geometric and dimensional synthesis of a component considering the constraints of the manufacturing process, such that the part emerges defect-free, with minimum material, and at the lowest total cost, while satisfying functional and service requirements."*

It is one of the three pillars of **Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA)**, sitting alongside:
1. **Material Selection**
2. **Process Selection**
3. **Form Design** ← *Our Focus*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chef and the Recipe" 🍲

Imagine you ask a chef to bake a cake. If your "design" says *"make a 5 kg spherical cake with sharp 90° corners and a hollow center,"* the chef will struggle:
- The corners will crack during cooling (just like a casting cracks at sharp corners).
- The hollow center will collapse (just like a molten weld pool collapses without support).
- The cake won't come out of the pan (just like a casting without draft angles gets stuck in the sand mold).

> **A good form design = A good recipe.** It tells the *manufacturing process* exactly what shape, transitions, and features will result in a **flawless, low-cost final product**.

| Domain | Casting Analogy | Weldment Analogy |
|--------|-----------------|------------------|
| Sharp corners → Stress concentration | Cracks form at hot spots during solidification | Stress risers at weld toes |
| Thick sections | Internal shrinkage cavities (voids) | Excessive heat input → distortion |
| Thin sections | Misruns / cold shuts | Burn-through of base metal |
| **Solution** | **Generous fillets, uniform sections, draft, ribs** | **Proper joint prep, weld size, sequencing** |

---

## 1.3 Why Study This in KTU 2024?

> [!NOTE]
> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping — KTU PERUT522:**
> - **CO1**: *Apply the principles of design for manufacturing and assembly to design components for various manufacturing processes.*
> - **Module 1 Weightage**: Foundational; appears in **ESE Part A (3 marks)** and **ESE Part B (14 marks)** frequently.

**Real-World Engineering Metrics Highlighted:**

- A **15% reduction** in casting weight (via hollow sections, ribs) can cut **material cost by ₹40–80 per kg** in industrial castings.
- A poorly designed weld joint can reduce fatigue life by **70–90%** compared to a properly designed one.
- **Draft Angle Standard**: Typically **1° to 3°** for sand castings; lower for die castings.
- **Shrinkage Allowance**: **1% to 2.5%** depending on the metal/alloy.

---

## 1.4 The Two Pillars of Form Design in This Module

### A. Castings (Subtractive Shape Formation by Solidification)
The molten metal flows into a cavity (mold) and solidifies. The designer must understand:
- **Fluid flow** of liquid metal
- **Heat transfer** during cooling
- **Solidification shrinkage** (~3–7% volumetric)
- **Mold material** interactions

### B. Weldments (Joining by Localized Fusion)
Two (or more) metal pieces are joined by melting a filler/base metal at the interface. The designer must understand:
- **Thermal cycles** (heat-affected zone - HAZ)
- **Residual stresses** from non-uniform heating/cooling
- **Joint accessibility** for the welding torch
- **Distortion control** through sequence and fixturing

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to Remember Both:** **"CAST-WELD"**
> **C** – Consistent thickness
> **A** – Avoid sharp corners
> **S** – Smooth transitions (fillets)
> **T** – Tapered surfaces (draft)
> **W** – Welds accessible & sequenced
> **E** – Edge prep correct
> **L** – Less material at non-critical zones
> **D** – Distortion anticipated & counteracted

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Form Design Principles for CASTINGS

### 2.1.1 Principle 1 — Uniform Wall Thickness

Molten metal cools **last** in the thickest section. If a thick section is connected to a thin one, a **hot spot** forms at the junction → **shrinkage cavity (void)** forms there.

**Design Rule:** Maintain section thickness as **uniform as possible**. When transition is unavoidable, use a **gradual taper** rather than an abrupt step.

$$
\text{Taper Ratio} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{L_{\text{transition}}} \quad \text{Recommended: } \leq 1:4 \text{ to } 1:6
$$

### 2.1.2 Principle 2 — Avoid Sharp Internal Corners (Use Fillets)

Stress concentrates at sharp re-entrant corners during:
- Solidification shrinkage
- Service loading

**Design Rule:** Provide **generous fillet radii** at all internal corners.

$$
r_{\text{min}} = 0.2 \times T \quad \text{(T = average section thickness)}
$$

For external corners, use **chamfers** instead of fillets to avoid mass concentration.

### 2.1.3 Principle 3 — Provide Adequate DRAFT ANGLE

A draft angle is the **slight taper** on vertical walls of a pattern so it can be withdrawn from the sand mold **without damaging** the mold cavity.

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{h}{D} \quad \text{(internal surface, D = cavity diameter)}
$$

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{h}{D - 2T} \quad \text{(external surface, T = wall thickness)}
$$

| Process | Typical Draft |
|---------|---------------|
| Sand casting | 1° – 3° |
| Die casting | 0.5° – 1.5° |
| Investment casting | 0° – 0.5° |
| Shell molding | 1° – 2° |

### 2.1.4 Principle 4 — Directional Solidification

The casting should be designed so that **solidification proceeds from the thinnest section toward the riser** (a reservoir of molten metal that feeds shrinkage).

**Design Rule:** Place the **riser at the heaviest (last-to-solidify) section**. Avoid "thermal islands" — isolated masses that solidify last without a feed path.

### 2.1.5 Principle 5 — Avoid Large Flat Surfaces

Large flat surfaces tend to:
- **Warp** due to uneven cooling
- **Cause mold deformation** (sand mold sags under the weight of liquid metal)

**Design Rule:** Slightly **crown or contour** large flats. Use **ribs and corrugations** for stiffness rather than adding thickness.

### 2.1.6 Principle 6 — Design the PARTING LINE Carefully

The parting line is the **interface between the two halves of the mold**. Best design:
- Lies on a **single plane** (preferably the symmetry plane)
- Is positioned to **minimize cores**
- Avoids complex curved parting lines (expensive tooling)

### 2.1.7 Principle 7 — Allow for Shrinkage

As molten metal solidifies, it **shrinks** (1–2.5% linearly). The pattern must be made **larger** by this amount.

$$
S = L \times s
$$

where $S$ = shrinkage allowance, $L$ = nominal dimension, $s$ = shrinkage factor.

| Metal | Linear Shrinkage (%) |
|-------|---------------------|
| Cast iron (gray) | 1.0 |
| Cast steel | 2.0 |
| Aluminum alloys | 1.3 |
| Brass | 1.5 |

---

## 2.2 Form Design Principles for WELDMENTS

### 2.2.1 Principle 1 — Welds Should Be Accessible and Visible

A welding torch needs **clear approach angles**. The designer must:
- Provide **minimum 50–75 mm** of clearance for the torch
- Position welds to allow **down-hand welding** whenever possible (gravity helps)
- Avoid welds in **enclosed or hidden** cavities (impossible to inspect and weld)

### 2.2.2 Principle 2 — Use the Strongest, Simplest Joint

**Joint strength hierarchy (increasing):**
1. Lap joint (fillet weld) — low strength
2. Tee joint (fillet weld) — moderate
3. Butt joint (square or single-V) — high
4. Butt joint (double-V or U-groove) — highest

**Design Rule:** **Use butt welds** for primary load-bearing members. Use **fillet welds** for attachments and secondary structures.

### 2.2.3 Principle 3 — Weld Size and Throat Thickness

For a **fillet weld** of leg size $s$ (the two equal sides of the right triangle):

$$
\text{Throat thickness } t_e = 0.707 \times s
$$

**Shear strength of fillet weld** (loaded parallel to the weld axis):

$$
P = 0.707 \times s \times L \times \tau_{\text{allow}}
$$

where $L$ = effective weld length, $\tau_{\text{allow}}$ = allowable shear stress.

For a **butt weld** of plate thickness $T$:

$$
P = T \times L \times \sigma_{\text{allow}}
$$

where $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$ = allowable tensile/compressive stress of weld metal.

### 2.2.4 Principle 4 — Edge Preparation

Thicker plates (typically **> 6 mm for steel**) require **groove preparation** for full penetration:

| Plate Thickness $T$ | Edge Preparation |
|---------------------|------------------|
| $T \leq 6$ mm | Square butt (no prep) |
| 6 mm < $T \leq 20$ mm | Single V-groove (60°–70° included angle) |
| $T > 20$ mm | Double V or U-groove |

### 2.2.5 Principle 5 — Minimize and Control Distortion

Distortion arises from **non-uniform thermal expansion and contraction**. Counter-design strategies:
- **Balanced welding** (weld on both sides of the neutral axis)
- **Backstep sequence** (weld in small segments opposite to the direction of progression)
- **Presetting** (deliberately misalign plates before welding so they straighten during cooling)
- **Use intermittent welds** for non-critical joints
- **Use fixtures and jigs** to hold parts in position

### 2.2.6 Principle 6 — Welds in Low-Stress Regions

**Design Rule:** Place welds in **regions of low stress** if possible. Avoid:
- Weld toes at **maximum bending moment** locations
- Welds crossing the **neutral axis** of bending members (they see max stress)
- Multiple welds intersecting at one point (creates a **3-D stress singularity**)

### 2.2.7 Principle 7 — Symbol-Based Communication

Weld symbols follow **ISO 2553 / AWS A2.4** standards. A weld symbol communicates:
- Type of weld (fillet, butt, plug, etc.)
- Size and length
- Spacing (for intermittent welds)
- Contour (flat, convex, concave)
- Field vs. shop weld

> **Example symbol interpretation**: A fillet weld symbol with **6** above and **100-50** below means a 6 mm fillet, 100 mm long, with 50 mm pitch (intermittent).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **master reference** for solving numerical problems in KTU exams. Memorize these.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Pattern Shrinkage Allowance | $S = L \times s$ | $L$ = length (mm), $s$ = shrinkage fraction | Casting pattern dimensioning |
| 2 | Draft Angle (Internal) | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{h}{D}$ | $h$ = depth, $D$ = diameter, $\alpha$ in degrees | Sand/die casting pattern design |
| 3 | Draft Angle (External) | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{h}{D - 2T}$ | $T$ = wall thickness | External pattern walls |
| 4 | Minimum Fillet Radius | $r_{\text{min}} = 0.2 \, T$ | $T$ = wall thickness | Internal corner of castings |
| 5 | Taper Ratio | $\dfrac{T_1 - T_2}{L_{\text{tr}}} \le \dfrac{1}{4}$ | $T_1, T_2$ = thicknesses, $L_{\text{tr}}$ = transition length | Section thickness transition |
| 6 | Fillet Weld Throat | $t_e = 0.707 \, s$ | $s$ = leg size | Fillet weld geometry |
| 7 | Fillet Weld Shear Strength | $P = 0.707 \, s \, L \, \tau_{\text{allow}}$ | $L$ = weld length, $\tau$ in MPa | Fillet weld load capacity |
| 8 | Butt Weld Tensile Strength | $P = T \cdot L \cdot \sigma_{\text{allow}}$ | $T$ = plate thickness | Butt weld load capacity |
| 9 | Volume of Filler Metal | $V = 0.707 \, s \, L$ | Per unit length | Weld metal estimation |
| 10 | Weld Metal Weight | $W = \rho \cdot V$ | $\rho$ = density (~7850 kg/m³ for steel) | Cost and material estimate |
| 11 | Effective Weld Length | $L_{\text{eff}} = L - 2 \, s$ | Subtract 2 × leg size at ends | Actual loaded length |
| 12 | Heat Input | $H = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{v} \times 0.06$ | $V$ = volts, $I$ = amps, $v$ = travel speed (mm/min) | Weld procedure planning |
| 13 | Distortion Angle | $\theta = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot Q}{c \cdot T^2}$ | $\alpha$ = thermal expansion, $Q$ = heat input, $T$ = thickness | Predicting weld distortion |
| 14 | Shrinkage Cavity Volume | $V_{\text{cav}} = \beta \cdot V_{\text{casting}}$ | $\beta$ = volumetric shrinkage fraction (0.03–0.07) | Riser sizing |
| 15 | Directional Solidification Check | $\text{Thermal gradient ratio: } G_R / G_M \ge 1$ | $G_R$ = gradient at riser, $G_M$ = at mold | Solidification verification |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | Application of Form Design Principles |
|-----------------|---------------------------------------|
| **Automotive** | Engine blocks (sand cast), chassis frames (welded) — both require uniform sections and accessible welds |
| **Aerospace** | Turbine blades (investment cast) with 0° draft; fuselage (welded Al-alloy) with backstep sequences |
| **Heavy Machinery** | Excavator booms — welded plate structures with stiffeners, balanced welding |
| **Pumps & Valves** | Cast iron housings with filleted junctions to avoid shrinkage porosity |
| **Pressure Vessels** | 100% butt welds with full penetration; X-ray inspected; welds away from nozzles |
| **Railways** | Bogie frames — fillet welds on T-joints with strict size & length symbols per ISO |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Every 1% reduction in casting weight via form design can save a **typical Indian SME foundry ₹30,000–50,000 annually per part** at production scales of 10,000+ parts.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shrinkage Allowance for a Casting Pattern

### Problem Setup
A gray cast iron gear blank has a nominal outer diameter of **300 mm** and a hub length of **150 mm**. The foundry data gives a linear shrinkage of **1.0%** for gray cast iron. Determine the dimensions to which the pattern must be made.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing relationship.**

The pattern must compensate for the linear shrinkage that occurs as the molten metal solidifies and cools to room temperature. The shrinkage is **linear** (one-dimensional) — the pattern is made proportionally larger by the shrinkage fraction.

$$
L_{\text{pattern}} = L_{\text{casting}} + S
$$

**Step 2 — Express the shrinkage allowance.**

$$
S = L_{\text{casting}} \times s
$$

where $s$ is the linear shrinkage fraction (here, $s = 0.01$ for gray cast iron).

**Step 3 — Substitute for the outer diameter.**

$$
S_D = 300 \, \text{mm} \times 0.01 = 3.0 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
D_{\text{pattern}} = 300 + 3.0 = 303.0 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute for the hub length.**

$$
S_L = 150 \, \text{mm} \times 0.01 = 1.5 \, \text{mm}
$$

$$
L_{\text{pattern}} = 150 + 1.5 = 151.5 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification by dimension type.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shrinkage is applied to ALL linear dimensions**: lengths, widths, heights, diameters, radii. It is **NOT** applied to non-dimension-dependent features (e.g., surface roughness, draft angle, fillet radius — these are handled separately).

**Result:**
- Pattern outer diameter: **303.0 mm**
- Pattern hub length: **151.5 mm**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Draft Angle for a Sand Casting Pattern

### Problem Setup
A cylindrical sand casting has an internal bore of **80 mm diameter** and a depth of **120 mm**. The standard draft angle for sand casting is **1.5°**. Determine:
(a) The taper on the diameter side
(b) The actual bore diameter at the top of the pattern (where the cope meets the pattern)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the draft angle formula for an internal surface.**

For an **internal** surface, the draft is measured on the inside of the cavity. The taper is applied such that the diameter *increases* as we go from the parting line downward.

$$
\tan(\alpha) = \frac{h}{D}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the tangent of the draft angle.**

$$
\tan(1.5^{\circ}) = 0.02618
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the taper $h$.**

$$
h = D \cdot \tan(\alpha) = 80 \times 0.02618 = 2.094 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the taper to the diameter.**

For an **internal bore**, the diameter **increases** as we go deeper (to ease pattern withdrawal):

$$
D_{\text{top}} = D_{\text{bottom}} - 2h = 80 - 2(2.094) = 75.81 \, \text{mm}
$$

(Wait — let me re-verify direction.) 

> **Clarification for Internal Cavity Pattern Withdrawal:**
> When the pattern is withdrawn from the sand mold, the cavity surface moves *upward*. To prevent the pattern from rubbing/damaging the cavity wall, the **cavity must be wider at the top (parting line) than at the bottom** — i.e., the **bore diameter at the top of the pattern is SMALLER** than the bore diameter at the bottom.

$$
D_{\text{top of pattern}} = D_{\text{nominal}} - h_{\text{taper}} = 80 - 2.094 = 77.91 \, \text{mm} \approx 77.9 \, \text{mm}
$$

> **Note**: The $2h$ factor would apply if we are varying the diameter and the depth on **both sides** of the bore. Since the draft angle is applied uniformly, the top diameter is reduced by $h$ on each side, total reduction $= 2h$. But the standard practice is to taper only **one side** (the deeper side from parting line), reducing the diameter at the parting line by $h$.

**Correct Standard Result:**

$$
D_{\text{at parting line}} = 80 - 2.094 = 77.91 \, \text{mm}
$$

This way, the pattern can be lifted straight up without scraping the sand wall.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Strength of a Fillet Welded Lap Joint

### Problem Setup
Two mild steel plates of thickness **10 mm** are joined by a **fillet weld on both sides**. The leg size of the weld is **8 mm**, and the effective weld length is **200 mm**. The allowable shear stress of the weld metal is **110 MPa**. Determine the load-carrying capacity of the joint.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the throat thickness (the shortest leg of the fillet triangle).**

The fillet weld is an isoceles right triangle (approximately) with leg size $s$. The hypotenuse — the **throat** — is the dimension through which the load is transferred.

$$
t_e = 0.707 \times s = 0.707 \times 8 = 5.656 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the load-transfer mechanism.**

In a **fillet weld loaded in shear parallel to the weld axis** (as in a lap joint), the failure plane is the throat. The shear stress acts on this plane.

$$
P = t_e \times L_{\text{eff}} \times \tau_{\text{allow}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute for a single fillet weld.**

$$
P_{\text{single}} = 5.656 \times 200 \times 110 = 124{,}432 \, \text{N} \approx 124.4 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for double-sided fillet weld.**

Since the joint has fillet welds on **both sides** of the lap, the total capacity doubles:

$$
P_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 124.4 = 248.86 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Step 5 — Check against the base metal shear capacity of the plates.**

The base metal (plate) must also be checked. For a lap joint, the plate itself is loaded in shear across its thickness:

$$
P_{\text{plate shear}} = T \times W \times \tau_{\text{plate, allow}}
$$

For mild steel, $\tau_{\text{plate, allow}} \approx 0.6 \times \sigma_y = 0.6 \times 250 = 150 \, \text{MPa}$ (assuming $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa for MS).

Assuming width $W = 200$ mm (matching the weld length):
$$
P_{\text{plate}} = 10 \times 200 \times 150 = 300{,}000 \, \text{N} = 300 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Step 6 — The governing capacity is the lesser of the two.**

$$
P_{\text{design}} = \min(248.86, 300) = 248.86 \, \text{kN}
$$

**Result:** The lap joint can safely carry approximately **248.9 kN** in shear.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Distortion Estimation in a Butt Weld

### Problem Setup
A low-carbon steel plate of thickness **12 mm** and length **2 m** is butt-welded. The heat input is **1.5 kJ/mm**, the thermal expansion coefficient is **$1.2 \times 10^{-5}$ /°C**, and the temperature rise is **assumed uniform across the weld zone**. Estimate the angular distortion.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Angular distortion formula (simplified Park's model).**

$$
\theta = \frac{0.5 \cdot \alpha \cdot Q}{T^2 \cdot c \cdot \rho} \quad \text{(in radians, simplified form)}
$$

A more practical form is:

$$
\theta = C \cdot \frac{\alpha \cdot Q}{T^2}
$$

where $C$ is a process constant (typically 0.1–0.2 for arc welding).

**Step 2 — Plug in numerical values.**

$$
\alpha = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{/°C}, \quad Q = 1.5 \times 10^3 \, \text{J/mm}, \quad T = 12 \, \text{mm}, \quad C = 0.15
$$

$$
\theta = 0.15 \times \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-5})(1500)}{(12)^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator.**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 1.2 \times 10^{-5} \times 1500 = 0.018
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} = 144
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the angle.**

$$
\theta = 0.15 \times \frac{0.018}{144} = 0.15 \times 0.000125 = 1.875 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{rad}
$$

Converting to degrees:

$$
\theta = 1.875 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 0.00107^{\circ}
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the **angular distortion per unit length** of the weld. For a **2 m long plate**, the **transverse shrinkage** (perpendicular to weld) is:

$$
\delta = L \cdot T \cdot \theta = 2000 \times 12 \times 1.875 \times 10^{-5} = 0.45 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Result:** The plate will distort by approximately **0.45 mm** at the free end. In practice, this is counteracted by **presetting, backstep welding, or restraint fixtures**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Casting Defects vs. Form Design Causes

| Defect Type | Symptom | Form Design Cause | Prevention by Design |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Shrinkage cavity** | Internal void | Thick section, no riser path | Directional solidification, riser placement |
| **Hot tear** | Crack at hot spot | Sharp internal corner, restrained contraction | Fillets, free contraction |
| **Cold shut** | Discontinuity on surface | Two streams of metal meet but don't fuse | Adequate gating, larger radii |
| **Misrun** | Incomplete filling | Section too thin, low pouring temp | Increase section thickness, preheat |
| **Sand inclusion** | Sand grain in casting | Weak mold, no draft | Higher draft, mold strength |
| **Warping** | Distorted casting | Large flat surface | Corrugation, ribs |
| **Gas porosity** | Holes inside | Sharp corners trap gas | Smooth flow paths, vents |

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Weld Defects vs. Form Design Causes

| Defect Type | Symptom | Form Design Cause | Prevention by Design |
|-------------|---------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Lack of penetration** | Weld doesn't reach root | No root gap, no edge prep | Proper V-groove, root gap |
| **Porosity** | Holes in weld | Contamination, poor accessibility | Clean surfaces, accessibility |
| **Undercut** | Groove at weld toe | Excessive current, poor joint fit | Proper current, edge alignment |
| **Distortion** | Plate bends | Unbalanced heat input | Backstep sequence, balanced welds |
| **Cracking** | Crack in HAZ | High restraint, brittle material | Lower restraint, preheat |
| **Incomplete fusion** | Weld doesn't bond to base | Low heat input, dirty surface | Adequate heat, clean surfaces |
| **Overlap** | Weld metal rolls over base | Too slow travel speed | Increase travel speed |

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Casting Form Design Decision Tree

The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the design decision sequence for a casting.

```mermaid

    A2 --> A3{Uniform Section Thickness?}
    A3 -->|No| A4[Add Tapered Transition: 1:4 to 1:6 Ratio]
    A3 -->|Yes| A5[Proceed to Corner Check]
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6{Sharp Internal Corners?}
    A6 -->|Yes| A7[Apply Fillet: r = 0.2 x Thickness]
    A6 -->|No| A8[Proceed to Draft Check]
    A7 --> A8
    A8 --> A9{Parting Line Defined?}
    A9 -->|No| A10[Set Parting Line: Single Plane Preferred]
    A9 -->|Yes| A11[Proceed to Shrinkage Allowance]
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12[Apply Shrinkage: S = L x s]
    A12 --> A13[Place Riser at Last-to-Solidify Section]
    A13 --> A14[Check Directional Solidification]

```


## 4.5 Weld Joint Geometry Reference

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1[Weld Joint Types] --> E2[Butt Joint: Square]
    E1 --> E3[Butt Joint: Single V]
    E1 --> E4[Butt Joint: Double V]
    E1 --> E5[Butt Joint: U Groove]
    E1 --> E6[Fillet Joint: Lap]
    E1 --> E7[Fillet Joint: Tee Corner]
    E1 --> E8[Fillet Joint: Edge]
    E2 --> E9[Use: Thin Plates T less than 6 mm]
    E3 --> E10[Use: 6 to 20 mm Plates]
    E4 --> E11[Use: Above 20 mm Plates Both Sides Accessible]
    E5 --> E12[Use: Thick Plates One Side Only]
    E6 --> E13[Use: Light Load Lap Connections]
    E7 --> E14[Use: Stiffeners and Brackets]
    E8 --> E15[Use: Sheet Metal Edge Connections]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Q: List any six form design guidelines for sand castings.**

**Model Answer (Expected Key Points):**

1. **Uniform wall thickness** to avoid shrinkage cavities at hot spots.
2. **Generous fillet radii** at all internal corners (r ≥ 0.2 T) to avoid stress concentration and hot tears.
3. **Adequate draft angle** (1°–3° for sand casting) on vertical surfaces for clean pattern withdrawal.
4. **Directional solidification** by placing risers at the heaviest/last-to-solidify section.
5. **Avoid large flat surfaces**; use ribs, corrugations, or crowning for stiffness.
6. **Single-plane parting line** positioned at the symmetry plane to minimize core usage.
7. **Compensate for shrinkage** by enlarging the pattern by 1%–2.5% linearly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** A 3-mark question expects **3–4 distinct guidelines with one-line justification each**. Writing only the guideline name without context leads to **partial marking**.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is draft angle? Why is it provided in patterns?**

**Model Answer:**

A **draft angle** is the small intentional taper provided on the vertical (or near-vertical) surfaces of a pattern so that it can be **withdrawn from the sand mold** without damaging or eroding the mold cavity walls.

**Why it is provided:**
- Prevents **damage to the mold cavity** during pattern withdrawal.
- Ensures **clean, accurate reproduction** of the pattern geometry in the casting.
- **Facilitates easy withdrawal** of complex patterns (especially with deep recesses).
- **Reduces scrap and rework** in the foundry.

**Typical values:**
- Sand casting: **1°–3°**
- Die casting: **0.5°–1.5°**
- Investment casting: **0°–0.5°**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Module carries a **14-mark question with internal choice (either/or)**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 1]

**Q: [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the various **form design principles to be followed in casting design** to obtain sound castings. List **at least seven principles**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A gray cast iron **rectangular block casting** has dimensions **300 mm × 200 mm × 50 mm**. If the pattern is to be made from wood with a shrinkage allowance of **1%** and a draft angle of **1.5°** on the vertical sides (200 mm and 300 mm sides), calculate:
(i) The pattern dimensions
(ii) The taper on each vertical face
(iii) The total length of the parting line if the casting has a single horizontal parting plane. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Seven Form Design Principles for Castings:**

1. **Uniform Wall Thickness:** Keep section thickness uniform throughout. Abrupt changes create hot spots → shrinkage cavities. Use **gradual transitions** with taper ratio ≤ 1:4.

2. **Generous Fillets at Internal Corners:** Internal corners should have a **fillet radius** of at least 20% of wall thickness (r = 0.2 T) to:
   - Reduce stress concentration
   - Allow smooth heat flow
   - Prevent hot tearing during solidification

3. **Adequate Draft Angle:** Provide taper on vertical walls:
   - **1°–3°** for sand casting
   - **0.5°–1.5°** for die casting
   This ensures clean pattern withdrawal.

4. **Directional Solidification:** The casting should solidify progressively from the **thinnest section toward the riser**. The riser must be the **last to freeze** so it can feed liquid metal to compensate for shrinkage.

5. **Avoid Large Flat Surfaces:** Use **ribs, corrugations, or crowned surfaces** to provide stiffness without adding bulk. This prevents warping and mold sag.

6. **Single-Plane Parting Line:** Position the parting line in a single plane (preferably the symmetry plane) to:
   - Simplify mold-making
   - Reduce core usage
   - Lower tooling cost

7. **Shrinkage Allowance:** Compensate for solidification shrinkage (1%–2.5%) by enlarging the pattern accordingly.

8. **Avoid Hot Spots:** Distribute metal mass via **ribs and webbing** rather than thick sections. Hot spots cause shrinkage porosity and cracks.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Pattern Dimensions (after shrinkage allowance):**

Given: shrinkage $s = 1\% = 0.01$

$$
L_{\text{pattern}} = L \times (1 + s)
$$

- Length (300 mm side): $300 \times 1.01 = 303.0$ mm
- Width (200 mm side): $200 \times 1.01 = 202.0$ mm
- Height/Thickness (50 mm side): $50 \times 1.01 = 50.5$ mm

**(ii) Taper on Vertical Faces:**

Draft angle = 1.5°. Using $\tan(1.5°) = 0.02618$:

- Taper on 300 mm side: $300 \times 0.02618 = 7.854$ mm
- Taper on 200 mm side: $200 \times 0.02618 = 5.236$ mm

**(iii) Parting Line Length:**

The parting line is the **perimeter of the casting at the parting plane**. If the parting plane is the **horizontal mid-plane** (i.e., at the 50 mm height level), the parting line traces the rectangular cross-section:

$$
P_{\text{line}} = 2 \times (300 + 200) = 1000 \, \text{mm} = 1.0 \, \text{m}
$$

> **Note:** If the casting is positioned with the 300 × 200 face as the parting plane, the parting line is the perimeter of that face = **1000 mm**.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 2]

**Q: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Discuss the **various form design considerations for weldments** to ensure sound welded joints. List **at least seven principles** with brief justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two mild steel plates of **thickness 15 mm** are joined by a **double-V butt weld** with an **effective weld length of 250 mm**. The ultimate tensile strength of the weld metal is **410 MPa**, and the joint efficiency is **80%**. Calculate:
(i) The tensile load-carrying capacity of the joint
(ii) The throat thickness of the V-groove if the groove angle is **60°** and the root face is **2 mm** (assume the V-groove is a complete joint penetration weld) **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Seven Form Design Considerations for Weldments:**

1. **Accessibility of Welds:** Provide at least **50–75 mm clearance** for the welding electrode/torch. Welds in enclosed cavities are difficult and unsafe.

2. **Weld Placement in Low-Stress Regions:** Position welds away from regions of **maximum bending stress** (i.e., avoid placing welds on the extreme fiber of bending members).

3. **Select the Strongest, Simplest Joint:** Prefer **butt welds** for primary load paths (full penetration, full strength). Use **fillet welds** for secondary attachments.

4. **Proper Edge Preparation:** Plates above 6 mm require **V or U grooves** for full penetration. Use **double-V** for plates above 20 mm to balance heat input.

5. **Minimize Distortion:** Use:
   - **Backstep sequence**
   - **Balanced welding** (both sides of neutral axis)
   - **Presetting** the plates
   - **Fixtures and jigs**

6. **Adequate Weld Size and Length:** Calculate the **throat thickness** and **effective length** to ensure the weld is at least as strong as the base metal (joint efficiency ≥ 80%–100%).

7. **Avoid Weld Intersections and Re-entrant Corners:** Multiple welds meeting at a point create a **3-D stress singularity**. Use **staggered welds** or **smooth transitions**.

8. **Standardized Weld Symbols:** All welds must be specified using **ISO 2553 / AWS A2.4** symbols for unambiguous communication.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Tensile Load Capacity:**

The strength of a butt weld (full penetration) is governed by:

$$
P = T \times L_{\text{eff}} \times \sigma_{\text{allow}}
$$

Allowable stress (with 80% joint efficiency and ultimate tensile strength of 410 MPa):

$$
\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \eta \times \sigma_u = 0.80 \times 410 = 328 \, \text{MPa}
$$

(Note: In design practice, the allowable stress is often based on yield strength divided by a safety factor. However, using joint efficiency as given, we use 80% of UTS.)

Substituting:

$$
P = 15 \times 250 \times 328
$$

$$
P = 1{,}230{,}000 \, \text{N} = 1230 \, \text{kN} = 1.23 \, \text{MN}
$$

**(ii) Throat Thickness of the V-Groove:**

For a single-V groove with:
- Included groove angle $\theta = 60°$
- Root face $b = 2$ mm
- Plate thickness $T = 15$ mm

The depth of the V-groove (excluding root face) is:

$$
d = T - b = 15 - 2 = 13 \, \text{mm}
$$

The **throat thickness** is the depth of the V minus the unfused portion at the root. For a complete joint penetration (CJP) butt weld, the effective throat is the full V-depth:

$$
t_e = \frac{d}{\cos(\theta/2)} = \frac{13}{\cos(30°)} = \frac{13}{0.866} = 15.01 \, \text{mm}
$$

However, the **actual depth of fusion** is $d = 13$ mm, which is what transfers the load. In many KTU problems, this is taken as:

$$
t_e \approx d = 13 \, \text{mm}
$$

(Use the plate thickness $T$ for full penetration butt weld: $t_e = T = 15$ mm, with root face ensuring full fusion.)

**Recommended Answer (Standard KTU Convention):**

$$
t_e = 15 \, \text{mm (full plate thickness for CJP butt weld)}
$$

> **Final Result:** The joint can carry **1.23 MN** in tension with a **throat thickness of 15 mm**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> **Mistake 1:** Students often forget to apply shrinkage in **both** directions of a dimension (e.g., length AND width of a rectangular block). The shrinkage is **linear and applies to every linear dimension**.
>
> **Mistake 2:** Confusing **draft angle on internal vs. external surfaces.** For an internal bore, the pattern diameter at the parting line is **smaller**; for an external surface, it is **larger**.
>
> **Mistake 3:** Using the **plate thickness** as the throat thickness for a **partial penetration** butt weld. For partial penetration, the throat is the **depth of the groove**, not the plate thickness.
>
> **Mistake 4:** Forgetting to **subtract $2s$** from the total weld length to get the **effective weld length** (the ends of a fillet weld have incomplete penetration due to the start/stop crater).
>
> **Mistake 5:** In casting design, students write "draft angle = 1°" without specifying whether it is **per side** or **total included angle.** Always clarify: "1° per side" is the standard.
>
> **Mistake 6:** **Not drawing sketches.** KTU examiners explicitly award **2–3 marks** for neat, labeled diagrams. Even a rough freehand sketch showing the section transition, fillet, or weld groove earns marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read This 5 Times Before Exam**

### Casting Form Design

✅ **Uniform wall thickness** — avoid abrupt changes; use taper ratio 1:4 to 1:6.
✅ **Fillet radius at internal corners** — $r = 0.2 \times T$ (T = wall thickness).
✅ **Draft angle** — 1°–3° for sand; 0.5°–1.5° for die; 0°–0.5° for investment.
✅ **Shrinkage allowance** — 1% (CI) to 2.5% (steel); pattern is **larger** by this amount.
✅ **Directional solidification** — riser at the **heaviest (last-to-freeze)** section.
✅ **Avoid large flat surfaces** — use ribs and corrugations.
✅ **Single-plane parting line** — at the symmetry plane; minimizes core use.
✅ **Chamfer (not fillet) at external corners** — prevents mass concentration.
✅ **Core prints** — adequate area and shape for core support.
✅ **Machining allowance** — extra material left on surfaces to be machined (typically 2–5 mm).

### Weldment Form Design

✅ **Prefer butt welds** for primary load paths; **fillet welds** for secondary.
✅ **Fillet weld throat** = $0.707 \times s$ (s = leg size).
✅ **Fillet weld shear capacity** = $0.707 \times s \times L_{\text{eff}} \times \tau_{\text{allow}}$.
✅ **Butt weld capacity** = $T \times L_{\text{eff}} \times \sigma_{\text{allow}}$ (for CJP).
✅ **Effective weld length** = total length − $2s$ (subtract crater length).
✅ **Edge prep** — single-V for 6–20 mm; double-V for >20 mm; U-groove for thick plate with one-side access.
✅ **Weld accessibility** — minimum 50–75 mm torch clearance.
✅ **Distortion control** — backstep, balanced welding, presetting, fixtures.
✅ **Welds in low-stress zones** — avoid welds at maximum bending moment.
✅ **Weld symbols per ISO 2553** — fillet symbol is a right triangle; butt symbol is a rectangle.

### Numerical Problem Patterns KTU Loves

📌 **Pattern dimensioning** with shrinkage + draft + machining allowance.
📌 **Fillet weld size** calculation for a given load.
📌 **Butt weld capacity** check against an applied load.
📌 **Heat input** calculation: $H = V \cdot I \cdot 0.06 / v$.
📌 **Distortion estimation** for a given plate thickness and heat input.
📌 **Riser sizing** based on shrinkage volume.

### Common Numerical Values to Memorize

| Constant | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Conversion factor 0.707 | $1/\sqrt{2}$ for fillet weld throat |
| Conversion 0.06 | Heat input factor (kJ/mm → J/mm·kJ units) |
| Joint efficiency (fillet) | ~70% of base metal |
| Joint efficiency (butt, CJP) | 100% of base metal |
| Shrinkage of cast iron | 1% (linear) |
| Shrinkage of cast steel | 2% (linear) |
| Draft for sand cast | 1°–3° |
| Plate thickness threshold for V-groove | 6 mm (mild steel) |

> 🎯 **Final Mantra for KTU Exam:**
> *"Cast: Uniform, Fillet, Draft, Riser. Weld: Accessible, Butt-Over-Fillet, Throat-Calculated, Distortion-Controlled."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process capability and metrics, cost aspects of tolerances
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process capability and metrics, cost aspects of tolerances

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Process Capability — The KTU Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Process Capability** is the measurable ability of a manufacturing process to consistently produce output that lies within the specified tolerance limits (Upper Specification Limit, USL, and Lower Specification Limit, LSL). It is expressed statistically as the ratio of the **tolerance band** (allowed design variation) to the **natural process spread** (actual variation the process exhibits under stable conditions).

In KTU 2024 scheme notation, a process is considered **capable** only when its natural 6σ spread comfortably fits inside the design tolerance window, and its mean is well-centered.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU Board Exams):**
> - **Machine Capability (Cm / Cmk)** — short-term study, no material/labor/operator variation, evaluates the *machine alone*.
> - **Process Capability (Cp / Cpk)** — long-term study, includes all sources of variation (machine, material, method, manpower, environment — the **5M's**).

### 1.2 Process Capability Indices — The Standard KTU Metrics

The indices **Cp** and **Cpk** are the universally accepted metrics under ISO 22514 and AIAG guidelines (followed in KTU valuation).

| Index | Full Name | What It Measures |
|-------|-----------|------------------|
| **Cp** | Process Capability (potential) | Spread capability only — ignores centering |
| **Cpk** | Process Capability Index (actual) | Spread **AND** centering combined |
| **Pp** | Process Performance | Overall (long-term) variation only |
| **Ppk** | Process Performance Index | Overall variation + centering |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The "Bucket and the Rain" Model:**
> Imagine the **tolerance window** as a *bucket*, and the **process output** as *rain falling on the bucket*.
> - **Cp** asks: *"Is the bucket wide enough to catch the cloudburst of variation?"*
> - **Cpk** asks: *"Even if the bucket is wide enough, is the cloudburst actually falling inside it, or is it missing to one side?"*
> - **Taguchi Loss** is the *wet floor around the bucket* — even rain landing *near* the bucket (within tolerance) causes some damage (quality loss). Only rain landing **exactly on target** is harmless.

A centered, well-spread process is like a bucket placed perfectly under the drain. A poorly designed tolerance (narrow bucket) is like trying to catch a monsoon in a teacup.

### 1.4 Cost Aspects of Tolerances — Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Tolerance-Cost Trade-off** is the *central economic dilemma* in Design for Manufacturing and Assembly (DFMA). Tighter tolerances exponentially increase manufacturing cost, but looser tolerances cause quality loss, assembly rework, and reduced product performance.

The KTU syllabus explicitly connects tolerance decisions to:
1. **Manufacturing cost** (machining, inspection, rejection)
2. **Quality loss cost** (Taguchi quadratic loss)
3. **Assembly cost** (mating, rework, interchangeability)
4. **Total cost of ownership** (warranty, recall, brand damage)

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Statistical Foundations — The Six Sigma Spread

A stable (in-control) process follows a **Normal (Gaussian) distribution** governed by two parameters:

- **Mean (μ)** — central tendency, the *target* of the process
- **Standard Deviation (σ)** — natural variability, the *process noise*

The fundamental properties used in capability analysis:

| Property | Coverage | Defect Rate (ppm) |
|----------|----------|-------------------|
| **μ ± 1σ** | 68.27% | 317,300 |
| **μ ± 2σ** | 95.45% | 45,500 |
| **μ ± 3σ** | 99.73% | 2,700 |
| **μ ± 4σ** | 99.9937% | 63 |
| **μ ± 5σ** | 99.99994% | 0.57 |
| **μ ± 6σ** | 99.9999998% | 0.002 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Six Sigma Methodology** sets the tolerance at **±6σ from the mean** (i.e., 12σ total spread), resulting in only **3.4 defects per million opportunities (DPMO)** when the process mean shifts by up to 1.5σ — the foundation of the Six Sigma quality standard.

### 2.2 The Two Core Capability Indices

**A) Cp — Potential Process Capability (Spread Only)**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{Tolerance Width}}{6\sigma} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

- Assumes the process is **perfectly centered** (μ = midpoint of tolerance).
- $C_p \geq 1.00$ → process is *marginally capable*.
- $C_p = 1.33$ → industry minimum acceptable (4σ each side).
- $C_p = 1.50$ → preferred for new processes.
- $C_p \geq 2.00$ → Six Sigma capable.

**B) Cpk — Actual Process Capability (Spread + Centering)**

$$C_{pk} = C_p \cdot (1 - k)$$

where **k** is the *centering index* (relative shift of the process mean from the tolerance midpoint):

$$k = \frac{\vert \text{Midpoint} - \mu \vert}{(\text{USL} - \text{LSL})/2} = \frac{2 \cdot \vert \text{Midpoint} - \mu \vert}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}$$

**Alternatively (the standard KTU form):**

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \;\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$$

- $C_{pk} = C_p$ → process is **perfectly centered** (k = 0).
- $C_{pk} < C_p$ → process is **off-center**.
- $C_{pk} < 1.00$ → process is producing **out-of-spec** parts, regardless of how wide the tolerance is.

### 2.3 The Taguchi Quality Loss Function (QFL)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Genichi Taguchi's revolutionary insight: **Quality loss is NOT a step function at the tolerance boundary.** Loss begins the moment the actual dimension deviates from the **target value** and increases **quadratically** with that deviation.

$$L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2$$

Where:
- $L(y)$ = quality loss per unit (in monetary terms, e.g., ₹)
- $y$ = actual measured characteristic
- $m$ = target value (the ideal, the *nominal*)
- $k$ = quality loss coefficient (in ₹ per unit² of deviation²)

**Determining k from a known boundary loss** $A_0$ at the tolerance limit $\Delta_0$:

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- At $y = m$ → $L = 0$ (perfect quality, zero loss).
- At $y = m \pm \Delta_0$ (at tolerance limit) → $L = A_0$ (maximum acceptable loss, e.g., cost of rework or warranty).
- Beyond tolerance → $L > A_0$ (scrap cost, customer loss, recall).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **This table is the KTU Board Exam "must-memorize" set.** All quantities carry consistent SI/metric units unless stated.

| # | Formula | Symbol Legend | Typical KTU Context |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Tolerance / 6σ | Spread capability |
| 2 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$ | Min of two one-sided indices | Actual capability |
| 3 | $k = \dfrac{2 \cdot \vert m - \mu \vert}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}$ | Centering ratio | Process shift |
| 4 | $C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$ | Potential × (1 - shift) | Compact form |
| 5 | $\text{DPMO} = \text{Defects} \times 10^6 / \text{Opportunities}$ | Defects/million | Six Sigma metric |
| 6 | $\text{Yield} = (1 - \text{Defect Rate}) \times 100\%$ | Percentage | Process efficiency |
| 7 | $L(y) = k (y - m)^2$ | Quadratic loss | Taguchi QLF |
| 8 | $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$ | Loss coefficient | From boundary data |
| 9 | $C_{\text{total}} = C_{\text{manuf}} + C_{\text{loss}} + C_{\text{assembly}}$ | Sum of cost components | Optimal tolerance |
| 10 | $\sigma = \bar{R} / d_2$ | Range-based estimate | Control-chart data |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace & Automotive**: Cpk ≥ 1.67 is mandatory for safety-critical features (engine blocks, brake calipers, turbine shafts).
- **Semiconductor industry**: Six Sigma (Cpk ≥ 2.0) is the entry-level, with many fabs operating at 4.5σ (3.4 DPMO) and pushing toward 5σ/6σ.
- **Medical devices**: Tolerance-cum-capability is used to set design limits that minimize Taguchi loss to the patient.
- **Mass manufacturing**: Optimizing tolerance via the cost-tolerance curve can save 5–15% of total product cost.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Cp and Cpk from First Principles

Let the design specification be **[LSL, USL]** and the actual process be characterized by a normal distribution $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

**Step 1 — Express the tolerance width:**

$$T = \text{USL} - \text{LSL}$$

This is the total allowable variation in the design.

**Step 2 — Express the natural process spread:**

The natural spread of a normally distributed process is conventionally taken as **±3σ** on either side of the mean, capturing **99.73%** of all output. The total natural spread is therefore:

$$S_p = 6\sigma$$

**Step 3 — Define Cp as the ratio of tolerance to spread:**

$$C_p = \frac{T}{S_p} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the centering offset:**

The midpoint of the tolerance band is:

$$m = \frac{\text{USL} + \text{LSL}}{2}$$

The actual distance from the mean to the upper and lower limits are:

$$d_{\text{upper}} = \text{USL} - \mu, \qquad d_{\text{lower}} = \mu - \text{LSL}$$

**Step 5 — Express Cpk as the minimum of two one-sided capabilities:**

$$C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\frac{d_{\text{upper}}}{3\sigma}, \; \frac{d_{\text{lower}}}{3\sigma}\right] = \min\!\left[\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \;\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$$

**Step 6 — Show equivalence with the compact form $C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$:**

Adding the two one-sided distances gives the total tolerance:

$$d_{\text{upper}} + d_{\text{lower}} = \text{USL} - \text{LSL} = T$$

The smaller one-sided distance is the limiting factor. If the process is shifted by $\delta = \vert \mu - m \vert$ from center, then:

$$d_{\text{upper}} = \frac{T}{2} - \delta \quad \text{and} \quad d_{\text{lower}} = \frac{T}{2} + \delta$$

(for the side that is closer). The minimum is $T/2 - \delta$. Substituting:

$$C_{pk} = \frac{T/2 - \delta}{3\sigma} = \frac{T}{6\sigma} - \frac{\delta}{3\sigma} = C_p - \frac{\delta}{3\sigma}$$

Recognising $k = 2\delta / T$ (relative shift), we have $\delta = kT/2$, and therefore:

$$C_{pk} = C_p - \frac{kT/2}{3\sigma} = C_p - \frac{kT}{6\sigma} = C_p - k \cdot C_p = C_p (1 - k)$$

This completes the derivation. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Cp and Cpk Calculation (KTU Typical)

> **Given:** A turned shaft has specification **24.00 ± 0.10 mm**. A sample of 50 measurements yields mean $\bar{x} = 24.04$ mm and standard deviation $s = 0.025$ mm. Find Cp, Cpk, and interpret.

**Step 1 — Identify limits and parameters:**

- USL = 24.10 mm
- LSL = 23.90 mm
- $\mu \approx \bar{x} = 24.04$ mm
- $\sigma \approx s = 0.025$ mm

**Step 2 — Compute Cp:**

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{24.10 - 23.90}{6 \times 0.025} = \frac{0.20}{0.15} = 1.333$$

**Step 3 — Compute one-sided indices:**

$$\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.10 - 24.04}{3 \times 0.025} = \frac{0.06}{0.075} = 0.800$$

$$\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = \frac{24.04 - 23.90}{3 \times 0.025} = \frac{0.14}{0.075} = 1.867$$

**Step 4 — Take the minimum:**

$$C_{pk} = \min(0.800, \; 1.867) = 0.800$$

**Step 5 — Centering index:**

$$k = \frac{2 \cdot \vert m - \mu \vert}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}} = \frac{2 \cdot \vert 24.00 - 24.04 \vert}{0.20} = \frac{0.08}{0.20} = 0.40$$

**Step 6 — Verification via compact form:**

$$C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k) = 1.333 \times (1 - 0.40) = 1.333 \times 0.60 = 0.800 \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Although $C_p = 1.33$ (industry-acceptable spread), the **Cpk = 0.80** reveals that the process mean has **drifted 0.04 mm above target**. The process is producing some parts that exceed the USL on the high side. Corrective action required: recalibrate the machine to bring $\mu$ back to 24.00 mm — this single adjustment can raise Cpk from 0.80 to 1.33.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Capability Interpretation Table

| Cpk Value | Sigma Level | Estimated PPM Defective | KTU Engineering Verdict |
|-----------|-------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| < 0.67 | < 2σ | > 45,500 | **Unacceptable** — process not capable, scrap rate very high |
| 0.67 – 1.00 | 2σ – 3σ | 45,500 – 2,700 | **Marginal** — needs improvement plan |
| 1.00 – 1.33 | 3σ – 4σ | 2,700 – 63 | **Acceptable** for non-critical features |
| 1.33 – 1.50 | 4σ – 4.5σ | 63 – 6.8 | **Preferred** for new processes |
| 1.50 – 1.67 | 4.5σ – 5σ | 6.8 – 0.57 | **Highly capable** — safety-critical threshold |
| ≥ 2.00 | ≥ 6σ | ≤ 3.4 | **Six Sigma world-class** |

### 3.4 Derivation of the Taguchi Loss Coefficient

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Setup:** A specification is $50.00 \pm 0.20$ mm. The customer's documented loss at the tolerance boundary (e.g., warranty claim, rework cost) is $A_0 =$ **₹ 400** per part. Find the Taguchi loss coefficient $k$ and the expected loss for a part measured at 50.05 mm.

**Step 1 — Identify the boundary deviation:**

At the tolerance limit, the deviation from the target $m = 50.00$ is:

$$\Delta_0 = 0.20 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the definition of the loss coefficient:**

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \frac{400}{(0.20)^2} = \frac{400}{0.04} = 10{,}000 \text{ ₹/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the loss for $y = 50.05$ mm:**

Deviation from target:

$$y - m = 50.05 - 50.00 = 0.05 \text{ mm}$$

Quadratic loss:

$$L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2 = 10{,}000 \times (0.05)^2 = 10{,}000 \times 0.0025 = \text{₹ } 25$$

**Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:**

> A part that is well inside tolerance (only 25% of the allowable deviation) still incurs ₹ 25 of "hidden" quality loss. This is the loss to society that traditional GO/NO-GO inspection completely misses — the foundation of Taguchi's argument for tighter tolerances and Six Sigma targeting of the nominal value.

### 3.5 Optimal Tolerance from Cost-Curves

The total cost of a tolerance choice is:

$$C_{\text{total}}(T) = C_{\text{manuf}}(T) + C_{\text{loss}}(T) + C_{\text{assembly}}(T)$$

- $C_{\text{manuf}}(T)$ → **decreases** as tolerance widens (looser specs are cheaper to make)
- $C_{\text{loss}}(T)$ → **increases** as tolerance widens (Taguchi loss grows with deviation)
- $C_{\text{assembly}}(T)$ → **increases** as tolerance widens (mating becomes difficult, more rework)

The optimal tolerance $T^*$ is found by setting the derivative to zero:

$$\frac{d C_{\text{total}}}{dT} = \frac{d C_{\text{manuf}}}{dT} + \frac{d C_{\text{loss}}}{dT} + \frac{d C_{\text{assembly}}}{dT} = 0$$

A widely used empirical (KTU Board-friendly) form for manufacturing cost:

$$C_{\text{manuf}}(T) = C_0 + \frac{K}{T^n}$$

where $C_0$ is the baseline cost, $K$ and $n$ are process-specific constants (typically $n = 1.5$ to $2.5$ for machining).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

```mermaid
    A --> C[Target Nominal m]
```
