<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
> **Define the terms (i) Process Capability, (ii) Cp, and (iii) Cpk. How does Cpk differ from Cp?**

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Process Capability:** It is the measure of the inherent ability of a stable manufacturing process to produce output within the design specification limits. It is a statistical concept expressed as the ratio of the tolerance band to the natural process spread.
>
> **(ii) Cp (Process Capability Index):** It is the *potential* capability of the process, considering only the spread. It is defined as:
>
> $$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$
>
> Cp assumes the process is **perfectly centered** at the midpoint of the tolerance.
>
> **(iii) Cpk (Process Capability Index — Actual):** It is the *actual* capability of the process, considering both spread and centering. It is the minimum of two one-sided indices:
>
> $$C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \;\frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right]$$
>
> **Difference:** Cp ignores whether the process mean coincides with the design nominal; Cpk penalizes any off-centering. For a centered process, Cpk = Cp; for a shifted process, Cpk < Cp. **(3 marks — full marks for complete definitions and the explicit difference.)**

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
> **Explain Taguchi's Quality Loss Function. Why is it preferred over the traditional "within tolerance is acceptable" view?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Taguchi's Quality Loss Function (QLF):** It is a quadratic function that quantifies the economic loss to society caused by deviation of a product's functional characteristic from its target value. It is given by:
>
> $$L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2$$
>
> where $k = A_0/\Delta_0^2$ is the loss coefficient determined from a known loss $A_0$ at the tolerance boundary $\Delta_0$.
>
> **Why it is preferred over the traditional view:**
>
> 1. The traditional view treats all parts within tolerance as equally good — a part at the nominal and a part at the limit are considered "OK." Taguchi shows that even a part at the limit causes measurable loss (rework, reduced life, customer dissatisfaction).
> 2. It expresses quality in **monetary terms**, making it directly comparable to manufacturing cost.
> 3. It drives the design philosophy toward *targeting the nominal*, not merely *staying inside the spec*, which is the foundation of Six Sigma and Robust Design.
> 4. It captures the *true* total cost of poor quality, including warranty, recall, and brand damage. **(3 marks — awarding full credit for the equation and the three reasoned points above.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**
>
> **(a)** The diameter of a shaft produced on an automatic lathe is specified as $40.00 \pm 0.08$ mm. From a sample of 100 shafts, the mean diameter is found to be 40.03 mm and the standard deviation is 0.02 mm.
>   - (i) Calculate the process capability indices Cp and Cpk. **(3 marks)**
>   - (ii) Comment on the capability of the process. Is the process capable? If not, what corrective action do you suggest? **(4 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A part has a specification of $25.0 \pm 0.5$ mm. The estimated loss to the customer at the tolerance limit is ₹ 800 per part. Calculate:
>   - (i) The Taguchi loss coefficient $k$. **(3 marks)**
>   - (ii) The expected quality loss for a part that measures 25.20 mm. **(2 marks)**
>   - (iii) Draw and explain the Taguchi Quality Loss Function curve for this specification. **(2 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Part (a)(i):**
> Given: USL = 40.08, LSL = 39.92, $\mu$ = 40.03, $\sigma$ = 0.02
>
> $$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} = \frac{40.08 - 39.92}{6 \times 0.02} = \frac{0.16}{0.12} = 1.333$$
>
> $$C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\frac{40.08 - 40.03}{3 \times 0.02}, \;\frac{40.03 - 39.92}{3 \times 0.02}\right] = \min\!\left[\frac{0.05}{0.06}, \;\frac{0.11}{0.06}\right] = \min(0.833, \;1.833) = 0.833$$
>
> **Part (a)(ii):**
> The **process spread is acceptable** ($C_p = 1.33 \geq 1.33$), but the **process is not actually capable** because $C_{pk} = 0.833 < 1.00$. The mean has shifted upward by 0.03 mm from the target 40.00 mm, so a fraction of parts exceed the USL.
>
> **Corrective action:** Recenter the machine — adjust the tool offset to bring the mean back to 40.00 mm. After recentering, Cpk will rise to 1.33, making the process fully capable. Additionally, the standard deviation can be reduced (better tooling, lower feed, vibration control) to further improve the margin.
>
> ---
>
> **Part (b)(i):**
> Given: $m = 25.0$, $\Delta_0 = 0.5$ mm, $A_0$ = ₹ 800
>
> $$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \frac{800}{(0.5)^2} = \frac{800}{0.25} = 3200 \;\text{₹/mm}^2$$
>
> **Part (b)(ii):**
> Deviation = $|y - m| = |25.20 - 25.00| = 0.20$ mm
>
> $$L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2 = 3200 \times (0.20)^2 = 3200 \times 0.04 = \text{₹ } 128$$
>
> **Part (b)(iii):**
>
> The Taguchi curve is a **parabola** with vertex at $(y, L) = (25.0, 0)$, opening upward. It passes through $(y, L) = (24.5, 800)$ and $(25.5, 800)$. Beyond these points, the curve transitions into the *exponential scrap region* where loss rises sharply due to warranty, recall, and customer dissatisfaction. The diagram highlights that loss starts immediately upon any deviation — the philosophical core of Taguchi's DFMA contribution.
>
> ![Taguchi Loss Function — Parabolic Curve from y = 24.5 to 25.5, vertex at 25.0]
>
> | $y$ (mm) | 24.5 | 24.8 | 25.0 | 25.2 | 25.5 |
> |----------|------|------|------|------|------|
> | $L$ (₹) | 800 | 128 | 0 | 128 | 800 |
>
> **(Total: 14 marks — full credit breakdown shown above.)**

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Alternative Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Define *Process Capability*. For a CNC turning operation on a pin of nominal diameter 30 mm, the upper and lower specification limits are 30.05 mm and 29.95 mm respectively. A capability study gives $\mu$ = 29.99 mm and $\sigma$ = 0.012 mm.
>   - (i) Calculate Cp and Cpk. **(4 marks)**
>   - (ii) If 50,000 pins are produced per month, estimate the number of defective pins per month using a standard normal table. **(3 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain in detail the **cost vs tolerance relationship** in manufacturing. With the help of a neat sketch, show how the *optimal tolerance* is determined. Why is "designing to the tightest tolerance" not always the best engineering decision? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

> **Part (a)(i):**
> Given: USL = 30.05, LSL = 29.95, $\mu$ = 29.99, $\sigma$ = 0.012
>
> $$C_p = \frac{30.05 - 29.95}{6 \times 0.012} = \frac{0.10}{0.072} = 1.389$$
>
> $$C_{pk} = \min\!\left[\frac{30.05 - 29.99}{3 \times 0.012}, \;\frac{29.99 - 29.95}{3 \times 0.012}\right] = \min\!\left[\frac{0.06}{0.036}, \;\frac{0.04}{0.036}\right] = \min(1.667, 1.111) = 1.111$$
>
> The process is **marginally capable** (Cpk between 1.00 and 1.33).
>
> **Part (a)(ii):**
> Compute the standard normal $z$-values for the two limits.
>
> For USL:
> $$z_{\text{USL}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{30.05 - 29.99}{0.012} = \frac{0.06}{0.012} = 5.00$$
> Tail probability beyond $z = 5.00$ is **0.000000287** (≈ 0.287 ppm) — effectively negligible.
>
> For LSL:
> $$z_{\text{LSL}} = \frac{\text{LSL} - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{29.95 - 29.99}{0.012} = \frac{-0.04}{0.012} = -3.33$$
> Tail probability beyond $z = -3.33$ is **0.00043** (≈ 430 ppm).
>
> Total defect rate = $0.287 + 430 = 430.3$ ppm ≈ 0.043%
>
> Defective pins per month:
>
> $$50{,}000 \times 0.000430 = 21.5 \approx 22 \text{ defective pins per month}$$
>
> ---
>
> **Part (b):**
>
> **Cost vs Tolerance Relationship:**
>
> Three cost components vary with the chosen tolerance width $T$:
>
> 1. **Manufacturing Cost** $C_m(T)$: An inverse function of $T$ — narrower tolerance demands finer machines, slower cutting, more setups, and 100% inspection, raising cost steeply. Empirical form: $C_m(T) = C_0 + K/T^n$, $n \approx 1.5$ to $2.5$.
>
> 2. **Quality Loss Cost** $C_q(T)$: A *direct* function of $T$ — wider tolerance increases Taguchi loss $k(y-m)^2$ on average. Wider bands allow larger deviations, increasing warranty cost, functional degradation, and customer dissatisfaction.
>
> 3. **Assembly Cost** $C_a(T)$: Also a *direct* function of $T$ — wider tolerances on mating parts cause more rework, selective assembly, and reduced interchangeability.
>
> **Total Cost:** $C_{\text{total}}(T) = C_m(T) + C_q(T) + C_a(T)$
>
> The optimal tolerance $T^*$ is the value of $T$ that **minimizes** $C_{\text{total}}(T)$, found from:
>
> $$\frac{d C_{\text{total}}}{dT} = 0 \quad \text{subject to} \quad T \geq T_{\text{functional}}$$
>
> where $T_{\text{functional}}$ is the minimum tolerance allowed by the product's functional requirement.
>
> **Sketch (described for KTU board):**
>
> The $C_m$ curve falls (convex) from top-left to bottom-right. The $C_q$ and $C_a$ curves rise (concave-up) from bottom-left to top-right. The $C_{\text{total}}$ curve is U-shaped with a minimum at $T^*$. The vertical line drawn from $T^*$ to the abscissa marks the optimal tolerance.
>
> **Why tightest is not always best:**
>
> - Tight tolerances drive up manufacturing cost **exponentially**, often without proportional gain in function.
> - The Taguchi loss within tolerance is often small compared to the manufacturing cost of holding the tolerance.
> - Over-tolerance specification wastes machine capacity, slows throughput, and increases inspection cost.
> - The *correct* engineering decision is to specify the **loosest tolerance consistent with the functional requirement** — captured by the famous principle **"specify the nominal, not the limit."**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 6 in Cp denominator.** Many students write $C_p = T / \sigma$ instead of $T / 6\sigma$. This is a **single-line, full-mark loss**.
> 2. **Confusing Cp and Cpk.** Cp is *spread only* (no centering); Cpk is *spread plus centering*. Examiners award zero if these are interchanged.
> 3. **Mis-applying the Taguchi equation.** Writing $L(y) = k \cdot y$ (linear) instead of $k(y-m)^2$ (quadratic) loses 2 of 3 marks on the QLF question.
> 4. **Failing to interpret Cpk values.** A numerical answer without a comment ("process is not capable because Cpk < 1") is treated as an incomplete answer.
> 5. **Ignoring the centering index k.** When asked for the *compact form*, students often skip the step $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Units mismatch.** Writing $k$ in ₹/mm but plugging in cm — always carry the units through.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**
>
> ✅ **Process Capability (Cp)** = Tolerance Width / 6σ — measures spread only.
>
> ✅ **Process Capability Index (Cpk)** = min[(USL-μ)/3σ, (μ-LSL)/3σ] — measures spread + centering.
>
> ✅ **Centering index (k)** = 2|m - μ| / (USL - LSL) — fraction by which the process mean has shifted from target.
>
> ✅ **Compact form:** Cpk = Cp(1 - k).
>
> ✅ **Six Sigma level** = 12σ total spread, 3.4 DPMO.
>
> ✅ **Taguchi Loss Function:** L(y) = k(y - m)²; loss coefficient k = A₀/Δ₀².
>
> ✅ **Quality loss is continuous** — there is no flat "OK zone" inside tolerance.
>
> ✅ **Process is capable** if Cpk ≥ 1.00; **highly capable** if Cpk ≥ 1.33; **safety-critical** if Cpk ≥ 1.67.
>
> ✅ **Total cost** = Manufacturing cost + Quality loss + Assembly cost → minimized at optimal tolerance T*.
>
> ✅ **Optimal tolerance principle** = loosest tolerance that satisfies functional requirement (minimum Taguchi loss + minimum total cost).
>
> ✅ **σ from range data:** $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R} / d_2$, where $d_2$ depends on subgroup size $n$ (e.g., $d_2 = 2.059$ for $n = 5$).
>
> ✅ **Pp and Ppk** = long-term (overall) analogues of Cp and Cpk; used when data come from the entire production run, not a controlled study.
>
> ✅ **Six Sigma shift convention** = 1.5σ mean shift; hence 6σ design tolerance yields 3.4 DPMO actual defect rate.
>
> ✅ **Practical KTU exam tip:** Always write the *units* of k (₹/mm²), Cpk (dimensionless), and σ (mm) explicitly in the final answer line.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feature and Geometric Tolerances: Understanding and applying tolerances in design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Feature and Geometric Tolerances: Understanding and applying tolerances in design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology) **Feature Tolerance** refers to the permissible variation in the *size* (dimensional limits) of an individual feature on a part. It defines the range between the **Maximum Material Condition (MMC)** and the **Least Material Condition (LMC)** within which the feature must be manufactured. Feature tolerances are governed by the **ISO System of Limits and Fits (IS 919 / ISO 286)**. **Geometric Tolerance (GD&T — Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing)** is the permissible variation in the *form, orientation, location,* and *runout* of a feature, independent of its size. GD&T is governed by **ASME Y14.5 (USA)** and **ISO 1101 (Europe/India — IS 13934 / IS 12102)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **DFMA Rule:** "Tolerances must be specified only when functionally required." Over-tolerancing inflates manufacturing cost exponentially, while under-tolerancing causes assembly failures. Feature and Geometric tolerances together form the **Tolerance Specification Pyramid** that bridges Design Intent and Manufacturing Reality. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine you are **building a wooden door and frame** for your house: - **Dimensional (Feature) Tolerance** → "The door panel width must be between **600 mm and 604 mm**." This is a *size* limit — easy to measure with a ruler. - **Geometric Tolerance** → "The door must hang so it is **flat (not warped)** within 0.5 mm over its length, and the hinges must be aligned **parallel to the floor** within 0.3°." These are *shape* and *orientation* limits — measured with special gauges, not rulers. A door that is dimensionally correct (right size) but **twisted or warped** will not close. Hence GD&T is essential. ## 1.3 Why Tolerances Matter in DFMA | Design Stage | Tolerance Decision | Impact | |---|---|---| | Conceptual | Allow generous tolerances | Low cost, may fail function | | Detailed | Optimize per functional need | Balanced cost/quality | | Manufacturing | Apply process capability | Final cost driver | > [!NOTE] > **Golden Principle of DFMA:** Every 1% tightening of tolerance typically increases manufacturing cost by **3–5%** (in machining); in precision grinding, costs may rise **10–20×** from IT11 to IT5. ## 1.4 Two Pillars of Tolerancing ``` TOLERANCING (Engineering Drawing) │ ├── FEATURE (Dimensional) Tolerance → IS 919 / ISO 286 │ • Size limits (Limits & Fits) │ • Based on Basic Size + Deviation │ └── GEOMETRIC (GD&T) Tolerance → ASME Y14.5 / ISO 1101 • Form, Orientation, Location, Runout, Profile • Based on Datum Reference Frame + Feature Control Frame ``` # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Feature Tolerances — ISO System of Limits and Fits ### 2.1.1 Terminology Hierarchy 1. **Basic Size (Nominal Size, D):** The reference size from which limits are derived (e.g., Ø50 mm). 2. **Deviation:** Algebraic difference between the limit and the basic size. - **Upper Deviation (ES for hole, es for shaft):** Upper Limit − Basic Size - **Lower Deviation (EI for hole, ei for shaft):** Lower Limit − Basic Size 3. **Tolerance (T):** Difference between upper and lower limits. 4. **Fundamental Deviation:** The deviation (upper for holes, lower for shafts) closest to the basic size. It defines the position of the tolerance zone. 5. **Tolerance Grade (IT Grade):** A letter designation (IT01, IT0, IT1 … IT18) defining the magnitude of the tolerance zone. IT01 = most precise, IT18 = coarsest. ### 2.1.2 Types of Fits | Fit Type | Description | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Clearance Fit** | Hole always larger than shaft (Min > 0) | Sliding gears, pulleys, bearings | | **Interference Fit (Force Fit)** | Shaft always larger than hole (Max < 0) | Permanent couplings, bushings | | **Transition Fit** | Either clearance or interference possible | Precision gears, keys,定位 pins | ### 2.1.3 Hole-Basis vs Shaft-Basis System - **Hole-Basis System (Preferred):** The hole is assigned the **H** designation (lower deviation EI = 0). Only the shaft tolerance varies. Reduces tooling cost (standard drills, reamers, broaches). - **Shaft-Basis System:** The shaft is assigned **h** (upper deviation es = 0). The hole tolerance varies. ### 2.1.4 Allowance (Minimum Clearance / Maximum Interference) $$\text{Allowance} = \text{Hole Lower Limit} - \text{Shaft Upper Limit}$$ ## 2.2 Geometric Tolerances (GD&T) — The Five Categories ### 2.2.1 Form Tolerances (No datum required) | Symbol | Name | Tolerance Zone | |---|---|---| | **—** | Straightness | Cylindrical/distance between two parallel lines/planes | | **▱** | Flatness | Space between two parallel planes | | **○** | Circularity (Roundness) | Space between two concentric circles (2D) | | **⌭** | Cylindricity | Space between two coaxial cylinders | ### 2.2.2 Orientation Tolerances (Datum required) | Symbol | Name | Reference | |---|---|---| | **∥** | Parallelism | Parallel to a datum plane/axis | | **⊥** | Perpendicularity | 90° to a datum plane/axis | | **∠** | Angularity | At a specified angle to a datum | ### 2.2.3 Location Tolerances (Datum required) | Symbol | Name | Reference | |---|---|---| | **⌖** | Position | True position defined by datums and dimensions | | **◎** | Concentricity/Coaxiality | Both axes share the same datum axis | | **⌯** | Symmetry | Mid-plane coincident with datum axis/plane | ### 2.2.4 Profile Tolerances | Symbol | Name | Reference | |---|---|---| | **⌒** | Profile of a Line | 2D curve lies within tolerance zone | | **⌓** | Profile of a Surface | 3D surface lies within tolerance zone | ### 2.2.5 Runout Tolerances | Symbol | Name | Reference | |---|---|---| | **↗** | Circular Runout | Single circular cross-section during one revolution | | **↗↗** | Total Runout | Entire surface during one revolution | ## 2.3 Datum Reference Frame (DRF) A **datum** is a theoretical reference point, line, or plane derived from a true geometric feature. Three mutually perpendicular primary, secondary, and tertiary datums form the **DRF (3-2-1 Principle)**: - **Primary (3 points):** Constrains 3 degrees of freedom (1 translation + 2 rotations) - **Secondary (2 points):** Constrains 2 more DoF (1 translation + 1 rotation) - **Tertiary (1 point):** Constrains final 1 DoF (translation) Total: **6 degrees of freedom** constrained — part is fully located. ## 2.4 Feature Control Frame (FCF) Structure ``` ┌──────┬────────────┬─────────┬──────────┬──────────┬──────────┐ │Symbol│ Tolerance │ Modifier│ Datum A │ Datum B │ Datum C │ │ │ Value │ (M/L/P) │(Primary) │(Secondary)│(Tertiary)│ └──────┴────────────┴─────────┴──────────┴──────────┴──────────┘ ``` **Example:** `⌖ | Ø0.5 Ⓜ | A | B | C` - Position tolerance of Ø0.5 mm at MMC, datums A-B-C. ## 2.5 Tolerance Modifiers | Modifier | Symbol | Meaning | Benefit | |---|---|---|---| | **MMC** | Ⓜ | Maximum Material Condition | Bonus tolerance available | | **LMC** | Ⓛ | Least Material Condition | Used for thin walls | | **RFS** | (none — default in ASME) | Regardless of Feature Size | No bonus | | **Projected** | Ⓟ | Extends tolerance above surface | For threaded fasteners | | **Free State** | Ⓕ | Applied to non-rigid parts | For flexible parts | ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Rule | Description | Standard | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T = \text{Upper Limit} - \text{Lower Limit}$ | Dimensional tolerance | IS 919 | | 2 | $\text{Allowance} = HL_{\min} - SL_{\max}$ | Min clearance / Max interference | IS 919 | | 3 | $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \ (\mu m)$ | Fundamental tolerance unit (D in mm) | ISO 286 | | 4 | $IT_n = k \cdot i$ | IT grade tolerance (k is grade factor) | ISO 286 | | 5 | $\text{Hole basis: } EI_{\text{hole}} = 0$ (for H) | Lower deviation of H hole = 0 | IS 919 | | 6 | $\text{Shaft basis: } es_{\text{shaft}} = 0$ (for h) | Upper deviation of h shaft = 0 | IS 919 | | 7 | $T_{\text{worst-case}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | Arithmetic (worst-case) stack-up | Tolerance analysis | | 8 | $T_{\text{RSS}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{\,2}}$ | Root Sum Squares (statistical) | Tolerance analysis | | 9 | $\text{Bonus Tolerance} = \vert \text{Actual size} - \text{MMC size}\vert$ | MMC bonus | ASME Y14.5 | | 10 | $\text{Total Position Tolerance} = \text{Geometric Tol} + \text{Bonus}$ | MMC allowed total | ASME Y14.5 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb:** RSS gives ~37% tighter tolerance than worst-case but assumes **random, statistically independent** variations — invalid if tolerances are functionally dependent or from a single process. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive (Engine Block):** Cylinder bore cylindricity → smoother piston motion. - **Aerospace (Turbine Disc):** Profile tolerance on airfoil → aerodynamic efficiency. - **Electronics (PCB Connectors):** Position tolerance of pins → ensures plug-fit with sockets. - **Hydraulics (Piston-Cylinder):** Roundness + parallelism → leak-tight sealing. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — IT Grade Tolerance from Fundamental Unit The ISO system defines the **Fundamental Tolerance Unit** *i* (in micrometres) for a given basic size *D* (in mm) in the range **0 < D ≤ 500 mm**: $$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \quad (\mu m) $$ **Reasoning behind the formula:** - The cube-root term reflects the empirical observation that **machining accuracy degrades as the cube root of size** (a part 8× larger needs only 2× the tolerance). - The linear term 0.001·D accounts for thermal, measurement, and elastic deformations that grow linearly with size. The tolerance for a specific IT grade *n* is: $$ IT_n = k_n \cdot i $$ where $k_n$ is a grade factor tabulated as: | IT Grade | 5 | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 | 10 | 11 | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | $k_n$ | 7 | 10 | 16 | 25 | 40 | 64 | 100 | ### Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** Find the tolerance for a Ø50 mm shaft at IT7 grade. **Step 1:** Compute *i* for D = 50 mm. $$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{50} + 0.001 \cdot 50 $$ $$ \sqrt[3]{50} = 3.684 $$ $$ i = 0.45 \cdot 3.684 + 0.001 \cdot 50 = 1.658 + 0.050 = 1.708 \ \mu m $$ **Step 2:** Apply $k_7 = 16$. $$ IT_7 = 16 \cdot 1.708 = 27.33 \ \mu m \approx 0.027 \ mm $$ **Step 3:** Interpretation. > A Ø50 mm shaft at IT7 has a tolerance zone of width **27 µm**, with the lower deviation set by the shaft designation (e.g., for shaft designation 'h', lower deviation = 0). ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hole-Shaft Limits and Fit Analysis **Given:** Basic size = Ø40 mm, Hole designation = **H8**, Shaft designation = **f7** (clearance fit). **Step 1:** Fundamental tolerance unit for D = 40 mm. $$ i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{40} + 0.001 \cdot 40 = 0.45 \cdot 3.420 + 0.040 = 1.539 + 0.040 = 1.579 \ \mu m $$ **Step 2:** Read IT grade tolerances from the table. For IT8: $k_8 = 25 \Rightarrow IT_8 = 25 \cdot 1.579 = 39.5 \ \mu m \approx 0.039 \ mm$ For IT7: $k_7 = 16 \Rightarrow IT_7 = 16 \cdot 1.579 = 25.3 \ \mu m \approx 0.025 \ mm$ **Step 3:** Apply fundamental deviations. - For hole **H**: lower deviation $EI = 0$. - For shaft **f** (clearance fit): upper deviation $es = -i \cdot k_f$. For shaft 'f' (lowercase letter), $es = -i \cdot 5 = -1.579 \cdot 5 = -7.9 \ \mu m \approx -0.008 \ mm$. **Step 4:** Calculate limits. Hole H8: $$ \text{Lower Limit} = 40.000 \ mm, \quad \text{Upper Limit} = 40.000 + 0.039 = 40.039 \ mm $$ Shaft f7: $$ \text{Upper Limit} = 40.000 - 0.008 = 39.992 \ mm $$ $$ \text{Lower Limit} = \text{Upper Limit} - IT_7 = 39.992 - 0.025 = 39.967 \ mm $$ **Step 5:** Calculate fit characteristics. $$ \text{Min Clearance} = HL_{\min} - SL_{\max} = 40.000 - 39.992 = 0.008 \ mm $$ $$ \text{Max Clearance} = HL_{\max} - SL_{\min} = 40.039 - 39.967 = 0.072 \ mm $$ > [!NOTE] > Since both clearances are **positive**, the fit is a **Clearance Fit (Sliding Fit)** — suitable for bearings or loosely coupled hubs. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Tolerance Stack-Up (Worst Case vs RSS) **Problem:** A shaft assembly has 5 components stacked in series, each with a tolerance of ±0.05 mm. Find (a) worst-case gap, (b) RSS gap. **Given:** $n = 5$, $T_i = \pm 0.05$ mm for all $i$. **(a) Worst-Case Method** — assumes all components simultaneously at extremes: $$ T_{\text{worst}} = \sum_{i=1}^{5} T_i = 5 \cdot (\pm 0.05) = \pm 0.25 \ mm $$ **(b) RSS Method** — assumes random, independent variation: $$ T_{\text{RSS}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{5} T_i^{\,2}} = \sqrt{5 \cdot (0.05)^2} = 0.05 \cdot \sqrt{5} $$ $$ T_{\text{RSS}} = 0.05 \cdot 2.236 = \pm 0.1118 \ mm $$ **Comparison:** $$ \frac{T_{\text{RSS}}}{T_{\text{worst}}} = \frac{0.1118}{0.25} = 0.447 \ (\text{i.e., } 55\% \text{ reduction}) $$ > [!WARNING] > RSS is **invalid** for *critical* dimensions (safety-related, sealing surfaces) — always use worst-case for such cases. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — MMC Bonus Tolerance Calculation **Given:** A hole with MMC = Ø20.0 mm, LMC = Ø20.4 mm, geometric position tolerance = Ø0.3 mm at MMC. The hole is manufactured at Ø20.2 mm. **Step 1:** Identify MMC and current size. $$ \text{MMC size} = 20.0 \ mm, \quad \text{Actual size} = 20.2 \ mm $$ **Step 2:** Calculate bonus tolerance. $$ \text{Bonus} = \vert \text{Actual size} - \text{MMC size} \vert = \vert 20.2 - 20.0 \vert = 0.2 \ mm $$ **Step 3:** Compute total allowed position tolerance. $$ T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{geometric}} + \text{Bonus} = 0.3 + 0.2 = 0.5 \ mm $$ > At Ø20.2 mm, the position tolerance zone expands from Ø0.3 to Ø0.5 mm — enabling easier assembly. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Geometric Tolerances ```mermaid flowchart TD A["GEOMETRIC TOLERANCES GD&T"] A --> B["FORM Tolerances
No Datum Required"] A --> C["ORIENTATION Tolerances
1 Datum Required"] A --> D["LOCATION Tolerances
Datums Required"] A --> E["PROFILE Tolerances
Optional Datums"] A --> F["RUNOUT Tolerances
Datum Axis Required"] B --> B1["Straightness -"] B --> B2["Flatness squareness sign"] B --> B3["Circularity o"] B --> B4["Cylindricity curved"] C --> C1["Parallelism parallel"] C --> C2["Perpendicularity perp"] C --> C3["Angularity angle"] D --> D1["Position pos"] D --> D2["Concentricity conc"] D --> D3["Symmetry sym"] E --> E1["Profile of a Line profLine"] E --> E2["Profile of a Surface profSurf"] F --> F1["Circular Runout cirRun"] F --> F2["Total Runout totRun"] ``` ## 4.2 Feature Control Frame (FCF) Anatomy ```mermaid block-beta columns 6 block:fcf S["SYMBOL"] T["TOL VALUE"] M["MODIFIER"] D1["DATUM A"] D2["DATUM B"] D3["DATUM C"] end style S fill:#FFD700 style T fill:#87CEEB style M fill:#90EE90 style D1 fill:#FFB6C1 style D2 fill:#FFB6C1 style D3 fill:#FFB6C1 ``` **Reading order:** `Symbol | Tolerance Value | Modifier | Primary Datum | Secondary Datum | Tertiary Datum` **Example Real FCF:** ``` ⌖ | Ø0.3 Ⓜ | A | B | C ``` Means: Position Ø0.3 mm at Maximum Material Condition, referenced to datums A (primary), B (secondary), C (tertiary). ## 4.3 Datum Reference Frame — 3-2-1 Principle ```mermaid flowchart LR Part["PART BLOCK"] Part --> P1["Primary Datum
3 Contact Points
Plane A (bottom)"] Part --> P2["Secondary Datum
2 Contact Points
Plane B (back)"] Part --> P3["Tertiary Datum
1 Contact Point
Plane C (side)"] P1 -.-> DOF1["Constrains 3 DoF
1 trans + 2 rot"] P2 -.-> DOF2["Constrains 2 DoF
1 trans + 1 rot"] P3 -.-> DOF3["Constrains 1 DoF
1 trans"] DOF1 --> TOTAL["Total: 6 DoF Locked
Fully Located"] DOF2 --> TOTAL DOF3 --> TOTAL ``` ## 4.4 Tolerance Specification Workflow in DFMA ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Step 1: Identify Functional Requirements"] Start --> S2["Step 2: Identify Critical Dimensions and Features"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Select Datums from Functional Surfaces"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Assign Dimensional Tolerances
Use Standard Fits"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: Assign Geometric Tolerances
Form / Orientation / Location"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Check Process Capability Cp, Cpk"] S6 --> Decide{"Process Capable?"} Decide -- No --> Reassign["Re-assign Wider Tolerance
or Select Better Process"] Reassign --> S4 Decide -- Yes --> S7["Step 7: Verify Stack-Up
Worst Case and RSS"] S7 --> S8["Step 8: Finalize Drawing and Release"] ``` ## 4.5 Tolerance Stack-Up — Worst Case vs RSS Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Component Tolerances
T1, T2, T3, ..., Tn"] A --> B["Assembled Gap
G = Σ xi"] B --> C{"Analysis Type?"} C -- Worst Case --> D["T_gap = Σ Ti
100 percent probability
Over-design"] C -- RSS --> E["T_gap = sqrt Σ Ti^2
99.73 percent probability
Statistical"] C -- Monte Carlo --> F["Simulation
10,000 trials
True distribution"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define the terms: (a) Basic Size, (b) Allowance, (c) Tolerance.** **Model Answer:** > **(a) Basic Size:** It is the reference size from which both upper and lower limits are derived by applying algebraic deviations. It is the nominal dimension used to designate the size of a feature (e.g., Ø50 mm). > > **(b) Allowance:** It is the intentional difference between the **minimum hole size** and the **maximum shaft size** in a fit. > > $$\text{Allowance} = HL_{\min} - SL_{\max}$$ > > - Positive value → **clearance fit** > - Negative value → **interference fit** > > **(c) Tolerance:** It is the total permissible variation in the size of a dimension, equal to the difference between the maximum and minimum limits. > > $$T = UL - LL$$ > > It is the *unavoidable manufacturing imprecision* allowed within functional limits. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **What is a Feature Control Frame (FCF)? Explain its components with a suitable example.** **Model Answer:** > A **Feature Control Frame (FCF)** is the rectangular symbol used on engineering drawings to specify geometric tolerances. It consists of **compartments read left to right**: > > 1. **Geometric Characteristic Symbol** (e.g., ⌖ for position, ∥ for parallelism) > 2. **Tolerance Value** (with prefix Ø if cylindrical zone) > 3. **Tolerance Modifier** (Ⓜ = MMC, Ⓛ = LMC, Ⓟ = Projected) > 4. **Primary Datum Letter** > 5. **Secondary Datum Letter** > 6. **Tertiary Datum Letter** > > **Example:** `⌖ | Ø0.2 Ⓜ | A | B` > means — Position tolerance Ø0.2 mm at MMC, referenced to primary datum A and secondary datum B. ## 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Modified]` **(a)** Explain the **ISO System of Limits and Fits** with neat sketches. Define *hole-basis* and *shaft-basis* systems and state which is preferred and why. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hole-shaft assembly has basic size **Ø60 mm**, with hole designation **H7** and shaft designation **k6**. Compute the limits, fits, and tolerance for both hole and shaft, given $i = 1.756 \ \mu m$ for D = 60 mm, $k_7 = 16$, $k_6 = 10$. Fundamental deviation for shaft k6: $es = +i \cdot k_{k6}$ where $k_{k6} = +10$. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) ISO System of Limits and Fits:** The ISO system (IS 919) standardizes tolerances and deviations using three parameters: 1. **Basic Size (D)** — The nominal size around which limits are derived. 2. **Tolerance Grade (IT)** — Letter designation IT01–IT18 indicating precision. 3. **Fundamental Deviation** — Single capital letter (H, K, M…) for holes or lowercase (h, k, m…) for shafts, defining the position of the tolerance zone. ``` ┌──── Basic Size Line ────┐ │ │ Upper ──┐ │ Tolerance Zone │ ┌── Upper │ │ (width = IT) │ │ Lower ───┤ │ │ ├── Lower └────┤ ├─────┘ └─────────────────────────┘ |<──── Hole ────>|<─── Shaft ──>| ``` - **Hole-Basis System:** Hole has designation **H** (lower deviation EI = 0). Shaft tolerance varies to obtain the desired fit. - **Shaft-Basis System:** Shaft has designation **h** (upper deviation es = 0). Hole tolerance varies. - **Preferred:** **Hole-Basis System** because standard cutting tools (drills, reamers, broaches) are made to a fixed size for H-holes, reducing tool inventory and cost. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute IT tolerances. $$ IT_7 = 16 \cdot 1.756 = 28.1 \ \mu m \approx 0.028 \ mm $$ $$ IT_6 = 10 \cdot 1.756 = 17.6 \ \mu m \approx 0.018 \ mm $$ **Step 2:** Hole H7 limits. For hole H: $EI = 0$. $$ \text{Lower Limit} = 60.000 \ mm $$ $$ \text{Upper Limit} = 60.000 + 0.028 = 60.028 \ mm $$ **Step 3:** Shaft k6 limits. For shaft k: $es = +i \cdot 10 = +1.756 \cdot 10 = +17.6 \ \mu m = +0.018 \ mm$. $$ \text{Upper Limit} = 60.000 + 0.018 = 60.018 \ mm $$ $$ \text{Lower Limit} = \text{Upper} - IT_6 = 60.018 - 0.018 = 60.000 \ mm $$ **Step 4:** Fit characteristics. $$ \text{Min Clearance} = HL_{\min} - SL_{\max} = 60.000 - 60.018 = -0.018 \ mm $$ $$ \text{Max Clearance} = HL_{\max} - SL_{\min} = 60.028 - 60.000 = +0.028 \ mm $$ **Conclusion:** Since Min Clearance is **negative (−0.018 mm = interference)** and Max is **positive (+0.028 mm = clearance)**, this is a **Transition Fit** — typically used for precision couplings, keys, and lightly loaded tight assemblies. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Most students confuse the formula $es$ for shaft letters. **Remember:** for shaft 'k', $es$ is **positive** (shaft larger than hole at upper limit). > - Always convert micrometres to mm before arithmetic. > - Do NOT swap H/k with h/K — capital = hole, lowercase = shaft. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Modified]` **(a)** Explain the **GD&T system** and its advantages over the traditional plus/minus tolerancing. List the **four geometric characteristic categories** with at least two symbols each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A shaft assembly consists of 4 components in series, with tolerances ±0.10, ±0.15, ±0.20, ±0.25 mm respectively. The target assembly gap is 50 ± 0.30 mm. Determine (i) Worst-case stack-up, (ii) RSS stack-up, and (iii) comment on process viability. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) GD&T System and Its Advantages:** **GD&T (Geometric Dimensioning and Tolerancing)** is a symbolic language (ASME Y14.5 / ISO 1101) that precisely defines the **form, orientation, location, and runout** of features using datums and feature control frames. **Advantages over ± Tolerancing:** | Aspect | ± Tolerancing | GD&T | |---|---|---| | Functional intent | Not clear | Explicitly stated | | Datum reference | Absent | Mandatory | | Tolerance zone | Rectangular (cubic) | Cylindrical / defined shape | | Bonus tolerance | None | MMC / LMC modifiers available | | Inspection method | Ruler / caliper | Functional gauges, CMM | | Cost vs function | Often over- or under-spec | Optimized per function | **Four Geometric Characteristic Categories:** 1. **Form:** Straightness, Flatness, Circularity, Cylindricity 2. **Orientation:** Parallelism, Perpendicularity, Angularity 3. **Location:** Position, Concentricity, Symmetry 4. **Runout:** Circular Runout, Total Runout (Profile is sometimes listed as a fifth category.) **(b) Tolerance Stack-Up Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $T_1 = 0.10$, $T_2 = 0.15$, $T_3 = 0.20$, $T_4 = 0.25$ mm. Target gap = 50 ± 0.30 mm. **(i) Worst-Case:** $$ T_{\text{worst}} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} T_i = 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.20 + 0.25 = 0.70 \ mm $$ $$ \text{Gap range} = 50 \pm 0.70 \ mm = [49.30, \ 50.70] \ mm $$ Since target gap tolerance is only ±0.30 mm, worst-case assembly range is **2.33× larger** — process is **NOT viable** by worst-case standard. **(ii) RSS:** $$ T_{\text{RSS}} = \sqrt{(0.10)^2 + (0.15)^2 + (0.20)^2 + (0.25)^2} $$ $$ = \sqrt{0.01 + 0.0225 + 0.04 + 0.0625} = \sqrt{0.135} = 0.367 \ mm $$ $$ \text{Gap range (RSS)} = 50 \pm 0.367 \ mm = [49.633, \ 50.367] \ mm $$ Still slightly above ±0.30 mm. **(iii) Comment:** - **Worst-case:** Not viable — tolerances must be tightened by selecting better processes or redistributing tolerances to non-critical features. - **RSS:** Borderline (22% over target) — process may work if statistical independence is verified, but recommended to tighten one or more tolerances by ~10%. - **Recommendation:** Apply **selective assembly** or **redesign the tolerance stack** by moving one tolerance to a sub-feature. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Many students forget the **square** inside the RSS sum — it is $T_i^{\,2}$, not $T_i$. > - Always specify whether the tolerance is **±** (half-range) or total. In this problem $T_i$ is total. > - If the question asks for "statistical" → RSS; if "guaranteed" or "worst" → arithmetic sum. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (DFMA — Feature & Geometric Tolerances)** - ☐ **Basic Size, Deviation, Tolerance** — memorize definitions and formulas. - ☐ **IT grades 5–11** are most common in mechanical parts; remember **i = 0.45·∛D + 0.001·D** in µm. - ☐ **Hole-basis (H)** is preferred globally; shaft varies. - ☐ **Clearance, Interference, Transition** fits — know identification logic. - ☐ **GD&T** is governed by ASME Y14.5 (USA) / ISO 1101 (Europe/India). - ☐ **Five categories:** Form, Orientation, Location, Profile, Runout. - ☐ **Datums:** Primary (3 pts) → Secondary (2 pts) → Tertiary (1 pt); total 6 DoF locked. - ☐ **Feature Control Frame** order: Symbol | Tolerance | Modifier | Datum A | Datum B | Datum C. - ☐ **MMC Modifier (Ⓜ)** = bonus tolerance available; **LMC (Ⓛ)** = bonus for thin walls. - ☐ **Worst-case stack-up** = ΣTᵢ; **RSS** = √(ΣTᵢ²); RSS is **only for independent, non-critical** dimensions. - ☐ **Rule of thumb:** Tighter tolerance → exponentially higher cost — specify only what is **functionally needed**. - ☐ **Process Capability** indices: $C_p = (USL-LSL)/(6\sigma)$, $C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu}, C_{pl})$; aim $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for established processes.

topics.title: Relationship between Tolerance Grades and Machining Processes: Attainable tolerance grades
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Relationship between Tolerance Grades and Machining Processes: Attainable tolerance grades

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **ISO System of Limits and Fits (ISO 286)**, the magnitude of the tolerance zone for any nominal dimension is governed by a **Tolerance Grade**, designated by the prefix **"IT"** followed by a number (e.g., **IT6, IT7, IT8**). There are **18 standard grades** designated as **IT01, IT0, IT1, IT2 … up to IT18**. 

A **tolerance grade** specifies *how wide* the tolerance zone is for a given nominal size, irrespective of whether the fit is a hole or a shaft. The numerical value of the standard tolerance (denoted by the lowercase letter **"i"**) is a function of the nominal diameter **D**, and is given by the **standard tolerance unit** formula:

$$i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$$

where **i** is in **micrometers (μm)** and **D** is in **millimeters (mm)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tolerance Grade ≠ Tolerance Value.** The grade (IT7) is just a label; the actual tolerance in mm depends on the nominal size of the part. For example, IT7 for Ø25 mm ≠ IT7 for Ø100 mm.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **packing a suitcase** for a trip:

- **Loose tolerance (IT12 – IT16):** You just throw clothes in roughly — the suitcase can hold anything that is close to its capacity. Cheaper, faster, but messy. This is like **sand casting** or **forging** — the part comes out roughly shaped.
- **Medium tolerance (IT7 – IT9):** You neatly fold and arrange clothes — they fit well but you don't measure each fold. This is like **turning, milling, or drilling** — standard machining.
- **Tight tolerance (IT01 – IT5):** Every item is measured, weighed, and precision-placed — costly and time-consuming. This is like **lapping, honing, or precision grinding** — used for bearings, gauges, and instrument parts.

The "tolerance grade" tells the machinist **how precisely** the process must control the dimension — and consequently, **which manufacturing process is economically capable** of achieving it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design for Manufacturing (DFM) Rule of Thumb:** Never specify a tighter tolerance grade than the process can reliably deliver at acceptable cost. A tolerance of **IT6** on a sand-cast part is **unmanufacturable**; a tolerance of **IT14** on a lapped surface is **over-specifying the process** (costing 10× more than necessary).

## 1.3 Standard Tolerance Grades — Quick Reference

| Grade Range | Classification | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **IT01 – IT1** | Ultra-precision | Gauges, instrument bearings |
| **IT2 – IT4** | Precision | Ball/roller bearings, master gauges |
| **IT5 – IT7** | High precision | Fits in machine tools, bearings |
| **IT8 – IT11** | Medium precision | General engineering fits |
| **IT12 – IT13** | Loose | Commercial tolerances |
| **IT14 – IT16** | Very loose | Castings, forgings, hot-rolled stock |
| **IT17 – IT18** | Extremely loose | Semi-finished products |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Standard Tolerance Unit (i)

The ISO system mathematically defines the smallest meaningful tolerance increment as a **function of diameter only** — not of the tolerance grade. The relationship was empirically derived so that the resulting values match historical national standards (B.S.A., ISA, DIN).

### Derivation Logic of "i"

The cube-root term $0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D}$ dominates for **small diameters** (D < 80 mm), while the linear term $0.001 \cdot D$ becomes significant for **large diameters** (D > 80 mm). This ensures that the same grade represents the *same relative accuracy* across the entire range of nominal sizes.

## 2.2 Tolerance Values for Each Grade

Each IT grade is a multiple of the standard tolerance unit **i**. The multipliers are fixed ISO constants:

| Tolerance Grade | Multiplier of "i" | Tolerance = Multiplier × i |
|---|---|---|
| **IT5** | 7i | $T_5 = 7i$ |
| **IT6** | 10i | $T_6 = 10i$ |
| **IT7** | 16i | $T_7 = 16i$ |
| **IT8** | 25i | $T_8 = 25i$ |
| **IT9** | 40i | $T_9 = 40i$ |
| **IT10** | 64i | $T_{10} = 64i$ |
| **IT11** | 100i | $T_{11} = 100i$ |
| **IT12** | 160i | $T_{12} = 160i$ |
| **IT13** | 250i | $T_{13} = 250i$ |
| **IT14** | 400i | $T_{14} = 400i$ |
| **IT15** | 640i | $T_{15} = 640i$ |
| **IT16** | 1000i | $T_{16} = 100i$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Grades IT01 through IT4 use slightly different multipliers (0.3 + 0.008·D form), but for KTU board exams, the above table covers **>95% of the syllabus**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Standard tolerance unit | $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$ | μm (D in mm) | Foundation of all ISO grades |
| Tolerance for grade ITn | $T_n = K_n \cdot i$ | μm | $K_n$ = ISO multiplier |
| Ratio $T_{n+1}/T_n$ | ≈ 1.6 | — | Geometric progression between grades |
| Surface roughness vs IT grade | $R_a \approx 0.1 \cdot T$ to $0.05 \cdot T$ | μm | Empirical correlation |
| Cost per unit tolerance | Doubles every 2 grades | — | Moore's economic rule |

## 2.4 Process Selection Logic — "Why" and "How"

### The Why
A manufacturing process has an inherent **process capability**, defined statistically as $\pm 3\sigma$ around the mean. The tolerance band must lie **inside** the process capability window — otherwise, defects (rejects) become unacceptably high.

### The How
1. **Identify** the functional requirement of the feature (fit? location? sealing?).
2. **Select** the IT grade that gives the required accuracy (e.g., sliding fit → IT7).
3. **Match** that grade to a process capable of delivering it economically.
4. **Verify** with a process capability study ($C_p, C_{pk} \geq 1.33$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The relationship is **bidirectional**: the *designer* selects a tolerance based on function, and the *manufacturer* confirms feasibility against process capability. If they conflict, **either the tolerance must be relaxed or the process upgraded**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive industry:** Crankshaft journals are ground to **IT5** because they must run at high RPM with hydrodynamic lubrication.
- **Civil engineering:** Concrete beams use **IT16+** because they are over-designed for safety.
- **Aerospace:** Turbine blade roots are machined to **IT4** by electrochemical grinding — a 10 μm error can cause a catastrophic failure.
- **DFMA Decision:** Selecting the right IT grade is the single biggest lever for **cost reduction** in mass production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Example 1 — Standard Tolerance Unit Calculation

**Problem:** Calculate the standard tolerance unit **i** for a nominal diameter **D = 50 mm**. Hence compute the tolerance for **IT8** grade.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Substitute D into the formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{50} + 0.001 \cdot 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the cube root:**

$$
\sqrt[3]{50} = 3.6840
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot 3.6840 + 0.05 \\
i &= 1.6578 + 0.05 \\
i &= 1.7078 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Round off to standard ISO table value:**

From the ISO standard table for the 30–50 mm size range, $i = 1.56 \; \mu m$ (table rounding). For KTU exam purposes, both values are accepted, but the **table value is preferred**.

**Step 5 — Compute IT8 tolerance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_8 &= 25i \\
T_8 &= 25 \cdot 1.56 \\
T_8 &= 39 \; \mu m = 0.039 \; mm
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** For a Ø50 mm shaft/hole, **IT8 tolerance = 0.039 mm (39 μm)**.

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — Selecting the Correct Machining Process

**Problem:** A shaft of nominal diameter **D = 80 mm** requires a tolerance of **±0.025 mm**. Determine (a) the standard tolerance unit, (b) the IT grade, and (c) the most economical machining process.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute i:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{80} + 0.001 \cdot 80 \\
\sqrt[3]{80} &= 4.3089 \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot 4.3089 + 0.08 \\
i &= 1.9390 + 0.08 \\
i &= 2.0190 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute total tolerance band:**

Total tolerance band = $2 \times 0.025 = 0.050 \; mm = 50 \; \mu m$.

**Step 3 — Determine IT grade:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_n &= K_n \cdot i \\
50 \; \mu m &= K_n \cdot 2.02 \; \mu m \\
K_n &= 50 / 2.02 \approx 24.75
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $K_{IT7} = 16$ and $K_{IT8} = 25$, the closest match is **IT8** (K = 25). Using IT8 standard table value: $T_8 = 25 \cdot 2.02 \approx 50 \; \mu m$ ✓

> **Answer:** The grade is **IT8**.

**Step 4 — Select the manufacturing process:**

From the process–tolerance relationship table (see Section 4), **IT8** is the typical working zone for:

- **Cylindrical turning** on a precision lathe
- **Milling** on a horizontal/vertical milling machine
- **Boring** on a horizontal boring machine
- **Broaching** (for through features)

The **most economical** choice for a Ø80 mm cylindrical shaft is **precision turning** on a CNC lathe with live tooling.

> **Answer:** Recommended process → **CNC Turning (IT8).**

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — Surface Roughness Correlation

**Problem:** A reamed hole of Ø30 mm has an IT7 tolerance. Estimate the expected surface roughness $R_a$.

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute i:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
i &= 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{30} + 0.001 \cdot 30 \\
\sqrt[3]{30} &= 3.1072 \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot 3.1072 + 0.03 \\
i &= 1.3983 + 0.03 \\
i &= 1.4283 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute tolerance for IT7:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_7 &= 16i \\
T_7 &= 16 \cdot 1.4283 \\
T_7 &= 22.85 \; \mu m \approx 0.023 \; mm
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Estimate surface roughness:**

Using the empirical rule $R_a \approx 0.05 \cdot T$ to $0.1 \cdot T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_a,min &= 0.05 \cdot 22.85 = 1.14 \; \mu m \\
R_a,max &= 0.10 \cdot 22.85 = 2.29 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** Expected $R_a$ range = **1.1 – 2.3 μm**, which is consistent with typical reamed surfaces (0.8–3.2 μm).

---

## 3.4 Example 4 — Cost Penalty for Over-Specification

**Problem:** A part is currently machined to **IT9** at a unit cost of ₹100. The designer requests **IT7** (2 grades tighter). Estimate the new cost using the economic rule "cost doubles every 2 grades tighter."

### Solution:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of grade steps tighter} &= IT9 - IT7 = 2 \text{ grades} \\
\text{Cost multiplier} &= 2^1 = 2 \text{ (since 2 grades tighter = 1 doubling step)} \\
\text{New unit cost} &= 100 \cdot 2 = ₹200
\end{aligned}
$$

If the designer instead demanded **IT6** (3 grades tighter from IT9):

$$
\text{Cost multiplier} = 2^{1.5} = 2.83 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{New cost} \approx ₹283
$$

> **Answer:** Every tightening of tolerance **doubles or more** the cost — a key DFM principle.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Tolerance Grade Selection Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Functional Requirement Identified] --> B{Type of Fit Needed}
    B -->|Clearance Fit| C{Load / Speed}
    B -->|Transition Fit| D[Locating Pins]
    B -->|Interference Fit| E[Press Fitted Components]
    C -->|Light / Low Speed| F[IT9 - IT11]
    C -->|Medium| G[IT7 - IT8]
    C -->|Heavy / High Speed| H[IT5 - IT6]
    F --> P1[Casting / Turning / Milling]
    G --> P2[Precision Turning / Milling / Boring]
    H --> P3[Grinding / Honing / Lapping]
    P1 --> Q[Verify Cp >= 1.33]
    P2 --> Q
    P3 --> Q
    Q --> R{Final Selection OK?}
    R -->|Yes| S[Release for Production]
    R -->|No| T[Relax Tolerance or Upgrade Process]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Process Capability vs Tolerance Grade Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L1[Ultra Precision Zone]
        A1[Lapping]
        A2[Superfinishing]
        A3[Precision Honing]
    end

    end

        D1[Die Casting]
        D2[Investment Casting]
        D3[Extrusion]

    end

        E1[Sand Casting]
        E2[Forging]
        E3[Rolling]
        E4[Sawing]
    end
    L1 -->|IT01 - IT5| F[Tolerance Grade Output]
    L2 -->|IT6 - IT8| F
    L3 -->|IT9 - IT11| F
    L4 -->|IT12 - IT14| F
    L5 -->|IT15 - IT18| F
```

## 4.3 Tolerance Grade vs Machining Process — Reference Matrix

| Process | Typical IT Grade | Typical $R_a$ (μm) | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lapping** | IT01 – IT3 | 0.05 – 0.4 | 100× |
| **Honing** | IT3 – IT5 | 0.1 – 1.0 | 30× |
| **Cylindrical Grinding** | IT4 – IT7 | 0.2 – 1.6 | 20× |
| **Surface Grinding** | IT5 – IT8 | 0.4 – 3.2 | 18× |
| **Broaching** | IT6 – IT8 | 0.4 – 3.2 | 15× |
| **Reaming** | IT7 – IT9 | 0.8 – 6.3 | 8× |
| **Precision Turning** | IT7 – IT9 | 0.8 – 6.3 | 5× |
| **CNC Milling** | IT8 – IT10 | 0.8 – 6.3 | 5× |
| **Boring** | IT8 – IT10 | 1.6 – 6.3 | 5× |
| **Drilling** | IT10 – IT12 | 3.2 – 12.5 | 2× |
| **Die Casting** | IT12 – IT14 | 1.6 – 12.5 | 1.5× |
| **Sand Casting** | IT14 – IT16 | 6.3 – 25 | 1× |
| **Forging (Hot)** | IT14 – IT16 | 6.3 – 25 | 1× |
| **Sawing / Shearing** | IT16 – IT18 | 25 – 100 | 0.5× |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1: Define "Tolerance Grade" as per the ISO system. How many standard tolerance grades are defined in ISO 286?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**
A **tolerance grade** is a letter/number designation that defines the *magnitude* of the tolerance zone for a given nominal dimension, independent of whether it is a hole or a shaft. The ISO 286 system defines **18 standard tolerance grades**, designated **IT01, IT0, IT1, IT2, ... IT18**, in order of increasing tolerance magnitude. IT01 is the tightest (precision instruments) and IT18 is the loosest (rough castings/rolled stock).

> *(Word count target: 60–80 words; 3 marks)*

---

### Question 2: Name any four manufacturing processes and the typical tolerance grade each can economically achieve.
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

| Process | Typical IT Grade |
|---|---|
| Lapping | IT01 – IT3 |
| Cylindrical Grinding | IT4 – IT7 |
| Precision Turning | IT7 – IT9 |
| Sand Casting | IT14 – IT16 |

> *(Tabular answer preferred; 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Question Bank]**

#### (a) Explain the ISO System of Tolerance Grades. Derive the standard tolerance unit "i" for a nominal diameter of 60 mm and compute the tolerance values for IT7 and IT8. (7 marks)

#### (b) Discuss the relationship between tolerance grades and machining processes. List at least six processes with their attainable IT grades. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of ISO Tolerance Grade:**
The ISO 286 standard defines a **tolerance grade** as the classification of the magnitude of the tolerance zone. There are 18 grades (IT01–IT18). The standard tolerance for each grade is derived from a common **standard tolerance unit "i"**, defined as:

$$
i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D \; \mu m \quad (D \text{ in mm})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute i for D = 60 mm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt[3]{60} &= 3.9149 \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot 3.9149 + 0.001 \cdot 60 \\
i &= 1.7617 + 0.0600 \\
i &= 1.8217 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the ISO standard table for the 50–80 mm range, $i = 1.86 \; \mu m$ (preferred).

**Step 3 — Compute IT7:**

$$
T_7 = 16i = 16 \cdot 1.86 = 29.76 \; \mu m \approx 0.030 \; mm
$$

**Step 4 — Compute IT8:**

$$
T_8 = 25i = 25 \cdot 1.86 = 46.5 \; \mu m \approx 0.046 \; mm
$$

**Conclusion:** For Ø60 mm, IT7 ≈ 0.030 mm and IT8 ≈ 0.046 mm.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

The relationship between tolerance grade and machining process is governed by the **process capability** — the inherent precision a process can repeatedly deliver. Tighter grades require more precise, expensive processes.

**Six processes and their attainable IT grades:**

| # | Process | Attainable IT Grade |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Lapping | IT01 – IT3 |
| 2 | Cylindrical Grinding | IT4 – IT7 |
| 3 | Surface Grinding | IT5 – IT8 |
| 4 | Broaching | IT6 – IT8 |
| 5 | CNC Turning / Milling | IT7 – IT10 |
| 6 | Drilling | IT10 – IT12 |
| 7 | Sand Casting | IT14 – IT16 |
| 8 | Hot Rolling / Forging | IT15 – IT18 |

The designer must choose the **loosest grade** that meets functional requirements to minimize manufacturing cost.

---

### Question B — Option 2 (Module Internal Choice)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

#### (a) Calculate the tolerance for a Ø40 mm hole/shaft under grades IT6, IT7, IT9, and IT11. Tabulate your results. (7 marks)

#### (b) "Selecting the correct IT grade is the single biggest cost lever in design for manufacture." Justify this statement with an example. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Compute i for D = 40 mm:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sqrt[3]{40} &= 3.4200 \\
i &= 0.45 \cdot 3.4200 + 0.001 \cdot 40 \\
i &= 1.5390 + 0.04 \\
i &= 1.5790 \; \mu m
\end{aligned}
$$

Standard ISO table value for 30–50 mm range: **i = 1.56 μm**.

**Step 2 — Compute tolerances for each grade:**

| Grade | Multiplier (Kₙ) | Tₙ = Kₙ × i | Tₙ (μm) | Tₙ (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **IT6** | 10 | 10 × 1.56 | 15.6 | 0.016 |
| **IT7** | 16 | 16 × 1.56 | 24.96 | 0.025 |
| **IT9** | 40 | 40 × 1.56 | 62.4 | 0.062 |
| **IT11** | 100 | 100 × 1.56 | 156 | 0.156 |

**Observation:** Each step of 2 IT grades roughly **doubles** the tolerance width (geometric progression with ratio ≈ 1.6).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Justification with Example:**

Consider a shaft of Ø50 mm:
- **IT11 grade** → tolerance 0.16 mm → achievable by **rough turning** → cost ≈ ₹50 per piece.
- **IT9 grade** → tolerance 0.062 mm → requires **precision turning** → cost ≈ ₹100 per piece.
- **IT7 grade** → tolerance 0.025 mm → requires **cylindrical grinding** → cost ≈ ₹250 per piece.
- **IT5 grade** → tolerance 0.011 mm → requires **lapping/honing** → cost ≈ ₹800 per piece.

**Inference:** Tightening by 6 IT grades (IT11 → IT5) increases cost by **16×**. Yet functionally, many parts need only IT9. Over-specification wastes lakhs in mass production.

Hence, choosing the **loosest functionally acceptable IT grade** is the most powerful cost-reduction lever in DFM — making the statement fully justified.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "tolerance grade" (ITn) with "tolerance value" (Tₙ in mm).** Many students write "IT7 = 25 μm" — wrong! IT7 is a *label*, not a value.
> 2. **Always quote units** (μm or mm) in the final answer. A bare number loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Use the ISO table value of i** rather than the formula value — the formula gives an approximate, the table is exact for the exam. (Both are accepted, but table value is preferred.)
> 4. **Don't mix hole/shaft terminology** with grade; IT grades apply equally to both.
> 5. **For Part B, always show the multiplier table (Kₙ values)** explicitly — many students skip this and lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> ### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist
> - [x] **ISO defines 18 tolerance grades:** IT01, IT0, IT1 ... IT18 (from tightest to loosest).
> - [x] **Standard tolerance unit formula:** $i = 0.45 \cdot \sqrt[3]{D} + 0.001 \cdot D$ (μm, D in mm).
> - [x] **Tolerance for grade ITn:** $T_n = K_n \cdot i$, where $K_n$ = 7, 10, 16, 25, 40, 64, 100, 160, 250 ... for IT5 onward.
> - [x] **Geometric ratio:** Each step up in grade multiplies tolerance by ~1.6×.
> - [x] **Process mapping (memorize the table):**
>   - **IT01 – IT3:** Lapping, Honing
>   - **IT4 – IT7:** Cylindrical & Surface Grinding
>   - **IT7 – IT9:** Precision Turning, Milling, Boring, Broaching
>   - **IT10 – IT12:** Drilling, Reaming
>   - **IT13 – IT16:** Die Casting, Sand Casting, Forging
>   - **IT17 – IT18:** Rolled Stock, Sawing
> - [x] **Surface roughness correlation:** $R_a \approx 0.05 T$ to $0.1 T$.
> - [x] **DFM golden rule:** Choose the *loosest* grade that meets functional requirements.
> - [x] **Cost rule:** Every 2 grades tighter ≈ doubles the manufacturing cost.
> - [x] **Process capability check:** Verify $C_p \geq 1.33$ before finalizing the grade.

> **End of Notes — Module 2 / Topic: Attainable Tolerance Grades**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cumulative Effect of Tolerances: Sure fit law, normal law, and truncated normal law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT522 - DESIGN FOR MANUFACTURING AND ASSEMBLY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cumulative Effect of Tolerances: Sure fit law, normal law, and truncated normal law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Cumulative Effect of Tolerances (Tolerance Stack-Up)

In any mechanical assembly, individual component dimensions are rarely independent. When several parts are stacked in a chain to form a critical assembly dimension (a **closed-loop dimension**), the individual tolerances **accumulate** and dictate the final functional clearance, interference, or position of the assembly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tolerance Stack-Up (KTU Definition):** The cumulative variation in an assembly dimension that results from the algebraic summation of the individual tolerances of the constituent components arranged in series along the dimension chain.

## 1.2 Three Fundamental Laws of Tolerance Accumulation

Engineering tolerances are commonly assessed using three statistical/arithmetic laws:

| # | Law | Engineering Significance |
|---|-----|--------------------------|
| 1 | **Sure Fit Law** (Arithmetic / Worst-Case) | Guarantees 100% interchangeability |
| 2 | **Normal Law** (Root Sum Square / RSS) | Realistic for mass production |
| 3 | **Truncated Normal Law** | Most realistic — accounts for scrapped parts |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Train Compartment Analogy:**
> Imagine 5 train compartments being coupled together. The **Sure Fit Law** says: "Assume every compartment is at its maximum stretched length (worst case), the total length is 5× the maximum." This is over-conservative — the train *will* fit the platform, but you used too much material.
> The **Normal Law** says: "In reality, only a few compartments will be at their extreme; most are near the mean." So the realistic total length is shorter.
> The **Truncated Normal Law** says: "Any compartment that exceeds its limit is *scrapped* before coupling." So the effective variability is even tighter.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics Used in KTU

- **Tolerance, T**: Total permissible variation in a dimension.
- **Standard Deviation, σ**: Measure of dimensional spread.
- **Mean Shift, m**: Difference between the actual mean and the nominal (target) value.
- **Confidence Level (kσ)**: Probability that the assembly dimension lies within limits (typically **k = 3** for 99.74% under normality).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Sure Fit Law (Arithmetic / Worst-Case Stack-Up)

### Core Concept
This law assumes the most pessimistic scenario: **every single component in the chain is produced at its extreme limit simultaneously**. The resulting assembly tolerance is the simple arithmetic sum of the individual tolerances.

### Logic Steps
1. Identify the **closed-loop (assembly) dimension** in the drawing.
2. List all individual tolerances in the chain as $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$.
3. Sum them arithmetically: this is the worst-case assembly tolerance.
4. The mean of the assembly is the sum of the individual means.
5. **Confidence:** 100% — every assembly will fit (but at the cost of tight component tolerances).

### Why & How
- **Why?** It eliminates the risk of assembly failure completely.
- **How?** By forcing each individual tolerance to be as tight as the cumulative sum divided by $n$.

## 2.2 The Normal Law (Root Sum Square, RSS)

### Core Concept
Real manufacturing processes produce dimensions that vary **randomly** about a mean, following a bell-shaped (Gaussian) distribution. Statistically, when independent random variables are added, their **variances** add (not their tolerances). The standard deviation of the assembly is the square root of the sum of the squared standard deviations.

### Logic Steps
1. Convert each tolerance to a standard deviation: $\sigma_i = T_i / 6$ (3-sigma rule, 99.74% confidence).
2. Sum the variances: $\sigma_{assy}^2 = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \ldots + \sigma_n^2$.
3. Take the square root to get $\sigma_{assy}$.
4. Multiply by 6 to obtain the 99.74%-confidence assembly tolerance.
5. **Confidence:** 99.74% — only 0.26% of assemblies will fall outside limits.

## 2.3 The Truncated Normal Law

### Core Concept
The Normal Law still allows 0.27% of components to fall outside their individual tolerance limits (in the tails of the Gaussian curve). In real production, such parts are **scrapped or reworked**. Thus the actual distribution is a *truncated* normal, confined strictly within $\pm T/2$. The arithmetic stays similar to the Normal Law, but the **interpretation** changes — there is no assumption of extreme tail behaviour.

### Logic Steps
1. Same RSS calculation as the Normal Law, but each $\sigma_i$ is treated as the standard deviation of a distribution **truncated at the tolerance limits**.
2. Often simplified to $\sigma_i = T_i/6$ (assuming the truncation point is at 3σ).
3. The mean of the truncated distribution shifts slightly toward the centre of the tolerance band.
4. **Confidence:** Essentially 100% within the *stated* tolerance, with 99.74% underlying Gaussian behaviour.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula sheet** for KTU Module 2 problems. Memorize the symbol conventions.

| Parameter | Sure Fit Law | Normal Law (RSS) | Truncated Normal Law |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Std. Dev. of component $i$** | $\sigma_i = T_i / 2$ | $\sigma_i = T_i / 6$ | $\sigma_i = T_i / 6$ |
| **Assembly Std. Dev.** | Not applicable (deterministic) | $\sigma_a = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$ | $\sigma_a = \sqrt{\sum \sigma_i^2}$ |
| **Mean of assembly** | $m_a = \sum m_i$ | $m_a = \sum m_i$ | $m_a = \sum m_i$ |
| **Assembly tolerance ($T_a$)** | $T_a = \sum T_i$ | $T_a = 6 \sigma_a = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$ | $T_a = 6 \sigma_a = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$ |
| **Confidence level** | **100%** | **99.74%** | ~**99.74% (effectively 100%)** |
| **Use when** | Low-volume, safety-critical | High-volume, statistical QC | High-volume, real-world QC rejects out-of-spec parts |
| **Tolerance severity** | Loosest components, tightest part tolerances | Tighter components, looser part tolerances | Same as Normal Law, more realistic interpretation |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Automotive assemblies** (engine blocks, gear trains): Normal / Truncated Normal Law.
- **Aerospace critical fits** (landing gear, turbine shafts): Sure Fit Law.
- **Consumer electronics** (laptop hinges, phone assemblies): Truncated Normal Law with 6-Sigma QC.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Sure Fit Law (Arithmetic Stack-Up)

Consider a closed-loop assembly formed by $n$ components stacked in series along an axis. Let each individual dimension be $X_i$ with tolerance $T_i$ and mean $m_i$.

The closed-loop (assembly) dimension is:
$$X_a = X_1 + X_2 + X_3 + \ldots + X_n$$

The upper limit of the assembly is:
$$X_{a,\max} = X_{1,\max} + X_{2,\max} + \ldots + X_{n,\max}$$

The lower limit is:
$$X_{a,\min} = X_{1,\min} + X_{2,\min} + \ldots + X_{n,\min}$$

The assembly tolerance is the difference:
$$T_a = X_{a,\max} - X_{a,\min} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_{i,\max} - X_{i,\min}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

Hence, the **Sure Fit Law** is derived as:
$$\boxed{T_a = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + \ldots + T_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i}$$

The mean of the assembly is:
$$m_a = m_1 + m_2 + \ldots + m_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Normal Law (RSS Method)

Each $X_i$ is treated as an **independent, normally distributed** random variable with mean $m_i$ and standard deviation $\sigma_i$.

For the sum of independent random variables, the **mean** is the sum of the individual means:
$$m_a = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$$

The **variance** of the sum equals the sum of the variances (a fundamental property of independent random variables):
$$\sigma_a^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2$$

Taking the square root:
$$\sigma_a = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2}$$

Using the **3-sigma rule** (where the total tolerance covers $\pm 3\sigma$, i.e., 99.74% of parts):
$$T_i = 6 \sigma_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_i = \frac{T_i}{6}$$

Substituting into the standard deviation equation:
$$\sigma_a = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{T_i}{6}\right)^2} = \frac{1}{6} \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2}$$

The assembly tolerance at 99.74% confidence:
$$T_a = 6 \sigma_a = 6 \cdot \frac{1}{6} \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2}$$

$$\boxed{T_a = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + T_3^2 + \ldots + T_n^2}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Truncated Normal Law

In a true normal distribution, the probability of a part falling outside $\pm 3\sigma$ is **0.27%**. In industry, such parts are **rejected (scrapped)**. The resulting distribution is therefore a **truncated normal** bounded at $\pm T_i/2$.

Let $f(x)$ be a normal PDF truncated at $\pm a$ where $a = T_i/2$. The new (truncated) variance is:
$$\sigma_{trunc}^2 = \sigma^2 \left[1 - \frac{2 f(a/a) \cdot a}{\Phi(a/\sigma)}\right]$$

For practical engineering purposes, the approximation **$\sigma_i = T_i/6$** is retained (corresponding to a truncation at 3-sigma limits, with 99.74% of the original distribution retained). The assembly tolerance then follows the same RSS form as the Normal Law, but the **interpretation is more realistic** — the tails beyond the limits are physically removed.

$$\boxed{T_a = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + \ldots + T_n^2} \quad \text{with 100% of accepted parts within limits}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** An assembly consists of 4 components stacked in series. The tolerances are: $T_1 = 0.20$ mm, $T_2 = 0.30$ mm, $T_3 = 0.15$ mm, $T_4 = 0.25$ mm. Calculate the assembly tolerance using all three laws.

### Solution — Sure Fit Law
$$T_a = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 = 0.20 + 0.30 + 0.15 + 0.25 = 0.90 \text{ mm}$$

### Solution — Normal Law
$$T_a = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + T_3^2 + T_4^2}$$
$$T_a = \sqrt{0.20^2 + 0.30^2 + 0.15^2 + 0.25^2}$$
$$T_a = \sqrt{0.0400 + 0.0900 + 0.0225 + 0.0625}$$
$$T_a = \sqrt{0.2150}$$
$$T_a = 0.4637 \text{ mm}$$

### Solution — Truncated Normal Law
Numerically identical to the Normal Law:
$$T_a = 0.4637 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(99.74% within limits; effectively 100% accepted)}$$

### Comparison Table

| Law | Assembly Tolerance | Reduction from Sure Fit |
|---|---|---|
| Sure Fit | **0.9000 mm** | — |
| Normal Law | **0.4637 mm** | **~48.5%** tighter |
| Truncated Normal | **0.4637 mm** | ~48.5% tighter (with 100% acceptance) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Tolerance Stack-Up Analysis Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Identify Assembly Closed-Loop Dimension] --> B[Extract Individual Component Tolerances T1 T2 ... Tn]
    B --> C{Determine Production Volume and Criticality}
    C -->|Low Volume OR Safety Critical| D[Apply SURE FIT LAW]
    C -->|High Volume AND Statistical QC| E[Apply NORMAL LAW RSS]
    C -->|High Volume AND Rejection of Out of Spec Parts| F[Apply TRUNCATED NORMAL LAW]
    D --> G[Sum Tolerances Arithmetically: T equals Sum of Ti]
    E --> H[Compute Std Dev: sigma equals Root of Sum sigma i squared]
    F --> I[Same as Normal Law with 100 percent Accepted Parts]
    G --> J[Compute Assembly Mean: m equals Sum mi]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Verify Against Functional Clearance or Interference Requirement]
    K --> L{Acceptable}
    L -->|Yes| M[FINAL DESIGN APPROVED]
    L -->|No| N[Redesign: Loosen Individual T or Add Adjustment Feature]
    N --> A
```

## 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram of the Three Laws

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LA[L01: SURE FIT LAW]
        SF1[Input n Tolerances Ti] --> SF2[Compute Sum T equals Sum Ti]
        SF2 --> SF3[Output Ta equals Sum Ti]
        SF3 --> SF4[Confidence equals 100 percent]
    end
    subgraph LB[L02: NORMAL LAW]
        NL1[Input n Tolerances Ti] --> NL2[Convert to Std Dev: sigma i equals Ti divided by 6]
        NL2 --> NL3[Compute Root Sum Square]
        NL3 --> NL4[Output Ta equals Square Root of Sum Ti squared]
        NL4 --> NL5[Confidence equals 99.74 percent]
    end
    subgraph LC[L03: TRUNCATED NORMAL LAW]
        TN1[Input n Tolerances Ti] --> TN2[Same RSS Formula]
        TN2 --> TN3[Truncate Tails at Limit plus minus T over 2]
        TN3 --> TN4[Output Ta equals Square Root of Sum Ti squared]
        TN4 --> TN5[Effective Confidence 100 percent Accepted]
    end
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Tolerance Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Production Scenario] --> Q1{Low Volume}
    P --> Q2{High Volume with SPC}
    P --> Q3{Critical Safety}
    Q1 --> R1[Use Sure Fit Law]
    Q2 --> R2[Use Truncated Normal Law]
    Q3 --> R1
    Q1 --> S1[Allocate Tightest Individual Tolerances]
    Q2 --> S2[Allocate Looser Tolerances to Save Cost]
    Q3 --> S1
    S1 --> OUT[Final Manufacturable Tolerance Stack]
    S2 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Visual Summary of Probability Distributions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SURE[S01: SURE FIT]
        SF_A[Rectangular Distribution] --> SF_B[All values from 0 to T equally likely]
    end
    subgraph NORM[S02: NORMAL]
        NL_A[Gaussian Bell Curve] --> NL_B[Mean at Centre, Tails extend to infinity]
        NL_B --> NL_C[99.74 percent within plus minus 3 sigma]
    end
    subgraph TRUN[S03: TRUNCATED NORMAL]
        TR_A[Truncated Bell Curve] --> TR_B[Cut off exactly at plus minus T over 2]
        TR_B --> TR_C[100 percent of accepted parts within limits]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the Sure Fit Law of tolerance stack-up. Under what circumstances is it preferred over statistical methods?**

**Model Answer:**
The Sure Fit Law, also called the **arithmetic** or **worst-case** law of tolerance accumulation, states that when $n$ components are stacked in series to form an assembly dimension, the **assembly tolerance is the arithmetic sum of all individual component tolerances**:

$$T_a = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + \ldots + T_n$$

It is preferred when:
1. The production volume is **low** (e.g., aerospace, defence equipment).
2. The assembly is **safety-critical** (e.g., brake assemblies, pressure vessels).
3. 100% interchangeability of parts is mandatory without statistical quality control.
4. The cost of failure far outweighs the cost of tighter component tolerances.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for applications)*

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
**Differentiate between the Normal Law and the Truncated Normal Law of tolerance stack-up.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Normal Law | Truncated Normal Law |
|---|---|---|
| **Distribution** | True Gaussian, extends to infinity | Gaussian with tails cut off at $\pm T/2$ |
| **Out-of-spec parts** | 0.27% lie outside $\pm 3\sigma$ | 0% — such parts are scrapped before assembly |
| **Confidence** | 99.74% (3σ rule) | Effectively 100% within stated limits |
| **Formula** | $T_a = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$ | Same RSS formula |
| **Realism** | Theoretical assumption | Closer to actual production reality |

*(3 marks: 1 for distribution, 1 for confidence, 1 for formula interpretation)*

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the assembly tolerance under the **Normal Law (RSS Method)**. State clearly the underlying assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A critical assembly dimension is formed by 5 components in series with the following tolerances: $T_1 = 0.10$ mm, $T_2 = 0.15$ mm, $T_3 = 0.08$ mm, $T_4 = 0.12$ mm, $T_5 = 0.20$ mm. Calculate the assembly tolerance using (i) Sure Fit Law and (ii) Normal Law. Comment on the percentage reduction. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Assumption 1:** Each component dimension $X_i$ is an **independent, normally distributed** random variable with mean $m_i$ and standard deviation $\sigma_i$.

**Assumption 2:** The total tolerance on each component covers $\pm 3\sigma_i$ (99.74% confidence), giving:
$$T_i = 6\sigma_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_i = \frac{T_i}{6}$$

**Derivation:** The closed-loop assembly dimension is the algebraic sum:
$$X_a = X_1 + X_2 + \ldots + X_n$$

The mean of the assembly:
$$m_a = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i$$

For independent random variables, **variances add** (not standard deviations):
$$\sigma_a^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2$$

Taking the square root:
$$\sigma_a = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^2} = \frac{1}{6}\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2}$$

At 99.74% confidence, the assembly tolerance:
$$T_a = 6\sigma_a = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + \ldots + T_n^2}$$

**QED.** *(7 marks: 2 for assumptions, 3 for derivation, 1 for final formula, 1 for confidence level)*

---

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Step 1 — Sure Fit Law:**
$$T_a = T_1 + T_2 + T_3 + T_4 + T_5$$
$$T_a = 0.10 + 0.15 + 0.08 + 0.12 + 0.20 = 0.65 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Normal Law:**
$$T_a = \sqrt{T_1^2 + T_2^2 + T_3^2 + T_4^2 + T_5^2}$$

Computing each term:
$$T_1^2 = 0.0100, \quad T_2^2 = 0.0225, \quad T_3^2 = 0.0064, \quad T_4^2 = 0.0144, \quad T_5^2 = 0.0400$$

Sum of squares:
$$\sum T_i^2 = 0.0100 + 0.0225 + 0.0064 + 0.0144 + 0.0400 = 0.0933$$

Square root:
$$T_a = \sqrt{0.0933} = 0.3055 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**
$$\text{Reduction} = \frac{0.65 - 0.3055}{0.65} \times 100\% \approx 53\%$$

**Comment:** The Normal Law gives a tolerance that is **~53% tighter** than the worst-case Sure Fit Law. This represents a **significant cost saving** in manufacturing, as individual component tolerances can be relaxed, reducing rejection rates. However, this comes with the trade-off that **0.26% of assemblies** may fall outside the limits. *(7 marks: 2 for Sure Fit, 3 for Normal Law calculation, 1 for percentage, 1 for engineering comment)*

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Explain the **Truncated Normal Law** of tolerance accumulation. How does it differ from the true Normal Law in real manufacturing environments? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shaft-hub assembly has the following mean shifts and standard deviations for 3 components: $m_1 = +0.02$ mm, $\sigma_1 = 0.03$ mm; $m_2 = -0.01$ mm, $\sigma_2 = 0.04$ mm; $m_3 = +0.03$ mm, $\sigma_3 = 0.02$ mm. Calculate the mean and standard deviation of the assembly. If the design clearance required is $0.00 \pm 0.10$ mm, determine the probability of assembly failure. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

The **Truncated Normal Law** is a modification of the Normal Law that accounts for the fact that in real production, **any component falling outside its tolerance limits is scrapped** and does not enter the assembly. The resulting distribution of accepted parts is a *truncated Gaussian*, bounded strictly at $\pm T_i/2$.

**Key Differences from the True Normal Law:**

1. **Distribution Shape:** The Normal Law assumes a true Gaussian curve with infinite tails. The Truncated Normal Law assumes the same shape but with the tails beyond $\pm T/2$ physically removed.

2. **Out-of-Spec Parts:** Under the Normal Law, **0.27% of parts lie outside $\pm 3\sigma$** and would theoretically enter the assembly. Under the Truncated Normal Law, such parts are **rejected at QC**, so **0% of accepted parts are out of limits**.

3. **Confidence Interpretation:** The Normal Law gives **99.74% confidence** of acceptance. The Truncated Normal Law gives **100% confidence within the stated limits** of accepted parts.

4. **Mean Behaviour:** In a truncated distribution, the **mean shifts slightly toward the centre** of the tolerance band, but in practice, the sum-of-means formula $m_a = \sum m_i$ is still applied.

5. **Practical Applicability:** The Truncated Normal Law is the most **realistic model for high-volume manufacturing** with established Statistical Process Control (SPC).

**Formula (unchanged in standard form):**
$$T_a = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2} \quad \text{with each } \sigma_i = \frac{T_i}{6}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for definition, 3 for differences, 2 for practical significance)*

---
